content: Personal Trainers should be able to describe the physiological and psychological benefits of a comprehensive exercise program. A comprehensive exercise program provides many physiological and psychological benefits. A well-rounded fitness plan includes a warm-up, the training stimulus, a cool-down, and flexibility exercise.  A comprehensive exercise program has many potential physiological as well as psychological benefits.
summary: This chunk outlines the benefits of a comprehensive exercise program, both physiological and psychological.

content: Personal Trainers should be able to describe the components of a comprehensive exercise program. The warm-up consists of at least 5–10 minutes of low- to moderate-intensity cardiorespiratory and muscular endurance activities. The conditioning phase consists of at least 20–60 minutes of aerobic, resistance, neuromotor, and/or sports activities. Exercise bouts of 10 minutes are acceptable if the individual accumulates at least 20–60 minutes per day of daily aerobic exercise. The cool-down consists of at least 5–10 minutes of low- to moderate-intensity cardiorespiratory and muscular endurance activities. Flexibility exercises should be performed for at least 10 minutes after the warm-up or cool-down phase.
summary: This chunk details the components of a comprehensive exercise program, including warm-up, conditioning, cool-down, and flexibility.

content: Personal Trainers should be able to consider advanced training options. When indicated, neuromotor exercise training may also be appropriate.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of considering advanced training options, such as neuromotor exercise.

content: Personal Trainers should be able to understand the anatomy of an exercise session.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the need for Personal Trainers to understand the structure of an exercise session.

content: Personal Trainers have the opportunity to assist clients in creating exercise programs that help to prevent several hypokinetic diseases. Hypokinetic diseases include coronary heart disease, obesity, diabetes, and low back pain.
summary: This chunk explains the role of Personal Trainers in preventing hypokinetic diseases through exercise programs.

content: The training stimulus can include cardiorespiratory exercise and/or resistance exercise.
summary: This chunk specifies the types of exercise that can constitute the training stimulus.

content: A well-rounded fitness plan includes a warm-up, the training stimulus, a cool-down, and flexibility exercise.
summary: This chunk reiterates the components of a well-rounded fitness plan.

content: In addition to health benefits, Personal Trainers should also be concerned with clients’ sedentary behavior. Sedentary behavior is associated with metabolic and cardiovascular risk factors. Sedentary behavior promotes morbidity and mortality and reduces life expectancy and quality of life. Detrimental effects of sedentary activities are possible even among individuals who meet the Physical Activity Guidelines. Including ‘physical activity breaks’ or frequent short activity bouts on a daily basis to interrupt sedentary activities is recommended. Detrimental effects of sedentary activities are possible even among individuals who meet the Physical Activity Guidelines. Including ‘physical activity breaks’ or frequent short activity bouts on a daily basis to interrupt sedentary activities is recommended.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of addressing sedentary behavior in addition to promoting physical activity.

content: Aerobic activities in which large muscle groups are used dynamically for extended periods of time place demand on the cardiovascular and respiratory systems in addition to the skeletal muscle system. By placing a stress on these systems, cardiorespiratory fitness can be improved. Increases in cardiorespiratory fitness are associated with a reduction in all-cause mortality. Conversely, low cardiorespiratory fitness is associated with an increased risk of premature death, in particular cardiovascular disease mortality.
summary: This chunk explains the link between aerobic exercise, cardiorespiratory fitness, and mortality risk.

content: Prevention of risk factors (primordial prevention) and treatment of risk factors (primary prevention) are both important considerations considering the high prevalence of heart disease. Some primordial risk factors cannot be altered or prevented (e.g., age, sex, or genetics), but others including physical inactivity can be addressed. Risk factors such as dyslipidemia, prediabetes, hypertension, and obesity are positively impacted with regular physical activity. Greater cardiorespiratory fitness in individuals with preexisting disease is associated with a decreased risk of clinical events.
summary: This chunk discusses the importance of both primordial and primary prevention of heart disease, highlighting the role of physical activity in addressing modifiable risk factors.

content: Physical activity and exercise are known to prevent the development of several life-threatening diseases as well as premature death. Morbidity refers to the amount of disease in a given population, whereas mortality refers to the amount of death in a population. All-cause mortality is delayed with regular physical activity. Mortality is also delayed when individuals who were previously sedentary or insufficiently active increase their physical activity to meet the recommended levels of physical activity.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the life-saving benefits of physical activity, reducing both morbidity and mortality.

content: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, each year, about 1 in 4 adults older than 65 years will experience a fall that leads to moderate or severe injury. Severe falls can cause injuries affecting mobility as well as brain trauma and can lead to other health conditions or disease. Falls may be linked to lack of muscular strength and endurance, balance, and coordination. Multicomponent physical activities (i.e., aerobic, muscle strengthening, balance, gait, coordination, and functional training) can help reduce the risk of injury from falls and improve physical function. Recreational activities such as dancing, yoga, tai chi, gardening, or sports can also be considered multicomponent because they often incorporate multiple types of physical activity.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of exercise for fall prevention in older adults, highlighting the benefits of multicomponent activities.

content: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, each year, about 1 in 4 adults older than 65 years will experience a fall that leads to moderate or severe injury. Falls may be linked to lack of muscular strength and endurance, balance, and coordination. Multicomponent physical activities (i.e., aerobic, muscle strengthening, balance, gait, coordination, and functional training) can help reduce the risk of injury from falls and improve physical function. Recreational activities such as dancing, yoga, tai chi, gardening, or sports can also be considered multicomponent because they often incorporate multiple types of physical activity. Neuromotor exercise training (also called functional fitness training), which includes balance, coordination, gait, agility, and proprioceptive training, is a beneficial component of a comprehensive exercise program for older persons to reduce the risk of falls.
summary: Falls are common in older adults and can be mitigated by multicomponent exercise programs that include balance, coordination, and functional training.

content: Metabolism is the rate at which bodily tissues break down and use energy consumed (calories). Unfortunately, metabolic rate declines steadily as a part of the aging process. Typically, as individuals age, they become less and less physically active, losing muscle mass, thus contributing to this steady decline in metabolic rate. Older adults should do muscle-strengthening activities that involve all the major muscle groups at least 2 d ∙ wk−1 performing two or three sets of 8–12 repetitions of each exercise in order to attenuate muscle loss or promote lean muscle gains, which can potentially maintain metabolic rate. More specifically, the effects of resistance training and especially the implementation of circuit training on energy expenditure is beneficial for weight control and cardiometabolic health in adults.
summary: Metabolic rate declines with age, but resistance training can help maintain muscle mass and combat this decline.

content: Bone health is affected by factors that cannot be modified, such as age, sex, race, and genetics, as well as those that can be changed, such as diet and physical activity. Exercise training modalities for increasing bone mass or slowing/preventing age-related bone loss require loading of the bone in a site-specific manner. Impact and weight-bearing activities, such as plyometrics, jumping, and resistance training, provide stress on the bone to promote positive adaptations.
summary: Bone health can be improved through weight-bearing exercises that place stress on the bones.

content: Obesity is associated with several chronic diseases, including coronary heart disease, hypertension, stroke, Type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, and some cancers. Physical activity is recommended as part of an integrated weight management plan for prevention of excessive fat mass, for weight maintenance, to enhance weight loss strategies, and to reduce the risk of weight gain with age. Evidence suggests a dose-response relationship between physical activity levels and the magnitude of weight loss. The American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) and the Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans recommend participation in at least 150 min ∙ wk−1 of moderate-intensity activity for weight maintenance and reduction in chronic disease risk. 250–300 min ∙ wk−1 of moderate-intensity activity may lead to greater weight loss.
summary: Physical activity is crucial for weight management and reducing the risk of chronic diseases associated with obesity.

content: Depression is a common psychiatric disorder and a leading cause of disability that is marked by feelings of sadness and unhappiness along with being self-critical and having low self-esteem. In addition to impairing daily function and potentially creating difficulties in work and home life, depression is also associated with poor quality of life and health risks such as heart disease, diabetes, cancer, and osteoporosis. Exercise (both cardiovascular and resistance training) has been found to be helpful in treating mild to moderate depressive symptoms, to potentially reduce the risk of developing depression, and to work in conjunction with medication-based antidepressive therapy for those with diagnosed major depression.
summary: Exercise can be beneficial for treating and preventing depression.

content: Anxiety is an emotional state marked by excessive anticipatory worry, tension, and apprehensive expectation or fear and is considered a common psychiatric symptom. Anxiety is associated with poor health-related quality of life and increased risk for cardiovascular disease and is linked to all-cause mortality. Exercise has been linked to reduction in anxiety and is considered an effective, affordable, and convenient treatment option for individuals with anxiety while improving a variety of physical fitness components. Potential physiological explanations for the link between exercise and reduced anxiety include regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, increases in serotonergic and noradrenergic levels in the brain, and endogenous opioid release. Exercise provides enhancement of self-esteem, more restful sleep, and faster recovery from psychosocial stressors. Exercise also has the potential to enhance emotional well-being, to improve mood, and to enhance feelings of ‘energy’ and quality of life.
summary: Exercise is an effective treatment for anxiety and can improve overall emotional well-being.

content: High stress is when the perceived demands appear to exceed the resources available to handle those demands. Stress is associated with a number of health risks, including weakening of the immune system, overeating, and adverse shifts in blood lipid levels. People with depression, those suffering from stress and hostility, have the same risk of heart attack as those who smoke or have high blood pressure. Exercise and physical activity have potentially positive effects on stress. For example, exercise training has been shown to reduce depression, overall stress, as well as hostility by 50%–70%.
summary: Exercise can help manage stress and reduce its negative health impacts.

content: Regular physical activity (both cardiovascular and resistance training) is known to play a pivotal role in the enhancement of cognitive function and reducing the risk for dementia or cognitive decline in older adults through processes of neuroplasticity. Cardiovascular exercise has been widely studied as an efficient and easily accessible training modality for remediation aiming to improve cognition. Multicomponent exercise programs promoting cardiorespiratory and muscular fitness, balance, and flexibility have been suggested as an effective training option to influence cognitive function and to be a part of a comprehensive approach for the prevention, management, and treatment of dementia in older adults. Exercise can also impact tasks that require complex mental processing, in particular, executive-control tasks such as coordination, inhibition, scheduling, planning, and working memory.
summary: Exercise, particularly cardiovascular and multicomponent programs, can improve cognitive function and reduce the risk of dementia.

content: The optimal exercise program should address the health-related physical fitness components of cardiorespiratory fitness, muscular strength and endurance, flexibility, body composition, and neuromotor fitness. Skill-related physical fitness components can also be added, and they include agility, coordination, balance, power, reaction time, and speed.
summary: A comprehensive exercise program should target all components of physical fitness, both health-related and skill-related.

content: Cardiovascular exercise has been widely studied as an efficient and easily accessible training modality for remediation aiming to improve cognition. Multicomponent exercise programs promoting cardiorespiratory and muscular fitness, balance, and flexibility have been suggested as an effective training option to influence cognitive function and to be a part of a comprehensive approach for the prevention, management, and treatment of dementia in older adults. Exercise can also impact tasks that require complex mental processing, in particular, executive-control tasks such as coordination, inhibition, scheduling, planning, and working memory.
summary: Cardiovascular exercise and multicomponent programs can improve cognitive function, particularly in older adults.

content: The optimal exercise program should address the health-related physical fitness components of cardiorespiratory fitness, muscular strength and endurance, flexibility, body composition, and neuromotor fitness. Skill-related physical fitness components can also be added, and they include agility, coordination, balance, power, reaction time, and speed. Some clients have goals related to sport or competitive activities in which the skill-related components need to be addressed.
summary: An optimal exercise program should encompass both health-related and skill-related fitness components, tailored to individual goals.

content: The FITT-VP principles of exercise prescription allow for the complete design of the frequency (F), intensity (I), time (T) (or duration), and type (T) (or mode) of exercise plus the overall volume (V) or amount and progression (P) of the exercise. Within the FITT-VP framework, the Personal Trainer can develop individual exercise prescriptions which include cardiorespiratory and muscular fitness exercises as well as flexibility-promoting activities.
summary: The FITT-VP principles guide the design of personalized exercise prescriptions, encompassing various fitness components.

content: Cardiorespiratory endurance refers to the ability of the heart and blood vessels (circulatory system) and the lungs (respiratory system) to provide oxygen to the body during sustained physical activity. Another term commonly used is ‘aerobic’ fitness because these activities require sufficient oxygen in order to be continued. Maximal oxygen consumption (V̇ O2max) can be measured directly by analyzing expired gases or may be estimated from submaximal effort or maximal effort. The higher the V̇ O2max, the greater is the individual’s aerobic capacity.
summary: Cardiorespiratory endurance, also known as aerobic fitness, is the ability of the body to supply oxygen during sustained activity, measured by V̇ O2max.

content: Cardiorespiratory endurance training can be done in two different ways: continuously or in blocks (intervals), such as high-intensity interval training or HIIT. The first training option consists of rhythmic, aerobic-type endurance exercises. The latter is characterized by high-intensity efforts and recovery bouts performed intermittently. Both options demonstrate similar improvements in cardiorespiratory fitness, but interval training generally is more time-efficient compared to steady-state, continuous bouts of exercise.
summary: Cardiorespiratory endurance training can be achieved through continuous or interval methods, both offering similar benefits with interval training being more time-efficient.

content: Although some activity is better than none, the recommended frequency for cardiovascular exercise is at least 3 d ∙ wk−1. When determining optimal frequency, intensity should be considered. As intensity levels increase, the number of days per week needed for health benefits decreases; the incidence of injury may also increase with vigorous intensity exercise done more than 5 d ∙ wk−1. In conclusion, a weekly combination of 3–5 d ∙ wk−1 of moderate- and vigorous-intensity exercise options can be performed, which may be more suitable for most individuals.
summary: Cardiovascular exercise frequency recommendations are at least 3 days per week, with intensity influencing the optimal number of days.

content: Exercise intensity can be quantified using various methods, including heart rate reserve (HRR), percentage of age-predicted maximal heart rate, oxygen uptake reserve (V̇ O2R), and rating of perceived exertion. The intensity recommended for a given individual depends on the person’s habitual activity and fitness level. For individuals who are sedentary and very deconditioned, the recommended intensity is very low (i.e., 30%–39% HRR or V̇ O2R) but progressively increases with higher activity and fitness levels (i.e., for habitually active individuals with high fitness, 60%–89% HRR or V̇ O2R).
summary: Exercise intensity can be measured using various methods and should be tailored to individual fitness levels, starting low for sedentary individuals and increasing with higher activity.

content: Typically, interval training can be classified as either HIIT or sprint interval training (SIT). HIIT, characterized by “near-maximal” efforts, is often performed at an intensity close to that which elicits ≥80%–100% peak heart rate; SIT is characterized by an all-out, supramaximal effort equal to or greater than the pace that elicits ≥100% peak heart rate.
summary: Interval training encompasses HIIT and SIT, both characterized by alternating high-intensity efforts with recovery periods, with SIT involving supramaximal efforts.

content: Exercise duration is the amount of time the exercise is performed, typically expressed as minutes per day or minutes per week. General baseline targets for time spent exercising depend on the intensity of the exercise. Thus, the recommendations link duration and intensity: Moderate-intensity exercise is recommended 30–60 min ∙ d−1 on at least 3 d ∙ wk−1 for a total of at least 150 min ∙ wk−1, Vigorous-intensity exercise is recommended 20–60 min ∙ d−1 on at least 3 d ∙ wk−1 for a total of at least 75 minutes, A combination of moderate- and vigorous-intensity exercise at least 20–30 minutes on 3–5 d ∙ wk−1
summary: Exercise duration recommendations vary based on intensity, with moderate-intensity exercise requiring 150 minutes per week and vigorous-intensity exercise requiring 75 minutes per week.

content: Interval training typically consists of alternating bouts of vigorous- to supramaximal-intensity exercise (20–240 s) followed by equal or longer bouts of light- to moderate-intensity exercise (60–360 s). However, for detrained individuals, a moderate-intensity interval training approach is recommended. For example, brisk walking periods are alternated with a reduced pace.
summary: Interval training involves alternating high-intensity and recovery periods, with a moderate-intensity approach recommended for detrained individuals.

content: A dose-response relationship exists between physical activity and health outcomes. Thus, benefits may begin at low levels for sedentary individuals (e.g., <20 min ∙ d−1), whereas extending the time or intensity may provide Additional health benefits may be gained by regular exercisers (5). One may gain even greater benefits with moderate-intensity exercise of 300 min ∙ wk−1, vigorous-intensity exercise of 150 min ∙ wk−1, or a combination of moderate- and vigorous-intensity exercise.
summary: Physical activity benefits increase with higher levels of time and intensity, with even greater benefits achievable with moderate-intensity exercise of 300 minutes per week or vigorous-intensity exercise of 150 minutes per week.

content: Neuromotor exercise is advocated for those at higher risk for falling, in particular, older individuals, although there are likely benefits for younger adults. Flexibility is recommended for everyone.
summary: Neuromotor exercise is beneficial for fall prevention, particularly in older adults, while flexibility is recommended for everyone.

content: A dose-response relationship exists between physical activity and health outcomes. Thus, benefits may begin at low levels for sedentary individuals (e.g., <20 min ∙ d−1), whereas extending the time or intensity may provide Additional health benefits may be gained by regular exercisers (5). One may gain even greater benefits with moderate-intensity exercise of 300 min ∙ wk−1, vigorous-intensity exercise of 150 min ∙ wk−1, or a combination of moderate- and vigorous-intensity exercise.
summary: Physical activity benefits increase with duration and intensity, starting at low levels for sedentary individuals and continuing for regular exercisers.

content: Going above the baseline of 150 min ∙ wk−1 of moderately intense exercise is important to assist with weight loss or to help maintain weight loss (5). In these situations, the recommended duration of exercise is 50–60 min ∙ d−1, or 250–300 min ∙ wk−1 (5).
summary: Increasing exercise duration beyond 150 minutes per week is crucial for weight management.

content: Examples of continuous cardiovascular activities include walking, jogging, running, cycling, swimming, and using endurance-based machines (e.g., stair climber, elliptical machines). Most of these activities require low to moderate skills and can be used even by those with low fitness levels, while others require considerable skill, fitness, and practice to master.
summary: Continuous cardiovascular activities range in skill level, from beginner-friendly options like walking to more advanced activities like swimming.

content: As fitness increases, more intense activities can be included (e.g., jogging). Other activities have a major skill component (e.g., swimming) that must be acquired before including in cardiovascular training or have a competitive nature (e.g., team sports) that requires at least average physical fitness (5).
summary: Exercise intensity and complexity can be adjusted based on fitness level, progressing from beginner-friendly options to more challenging activities.

content: An interval training approach incorporates aerobic-based (e.g., running, cycling, and rowing), resistance-based (e.g., bodyweight exercises, plyometrics, traditional or adjunct resistance training equipment), or a hybrid of both aerobic- and resistance-based exercises depending on the goals of the training session and physical fitness level of the client (5).
summary: Interval training can combine aerobic and resistance exercises, tailored to individual goals and fitness levels.

content: However, for detrained individuals, a moderate-intensity interval training approach is recommended. For example, brisk walking periods are alternated with a reduced pace.
summary: Detrained individuals benefit from moderate-intensity interval training, such as alternating brisk walking with slower paces.

content: Exercise volume plays an important role for realizing health/fitness outcomes, particularly with respect to body composition and weight management. Exercise volume should be used to estimate the overall energy expenditure for an exercise prescription. Exercise volume is typically measured in metabolic equivalent (MET) ∙ min ∙ wk−1 and/or kcal ∙ wk−1 (note that volume can also be tracked on a daily basis).
summary: Exercise volume, measured in METs or kcal, is crucial for achieving health and fitness goals, especially for body composition and weight management.

content: The recommended volume that is consistently associated with lower rates of cardiovascular disease and premature mortality is greater than 500–1,000 MET ∙ min ∙ wk−1 (11). This is approximately equal to 1,000 kcal ∙ wk−1 of moderate-intensity physical activity, ~150 min ∙ wk−1 of moderate-intensity exercise, an intensity of 3–5.9 METs (for people weighing 68–91 kg or 150–200 lb), or 75 min ∙ wk−1 of vigorous-intensity aerobic exercise, or an equivalent combination of moderate- and vigorous-intensity cardiovascular exercise per week to attain the volume of recommended physical activity.
summary: A recommended exercise volume of over 500-1000 METs per week is linked to reduced cardiovascular disease risk.

content: In deconditioned people, lower exercise volumes can have significant benefits, but even greater volumes may be needed for weight management.
summary: Exercise volume benefits deconditioned individuals, but higher volumes may be needed for weight management.

content: Metabolic equivalents (METs): An index of energy expenditure. “[A MET is] the ratio of the rate of energy expended during an activity to the rate of energy expended at rest. . . . [One] MET is the rate of energy expenditure while sitting at rest . . . by convention, [1 MET is equal to] an oxygen uptake of 3.5 [mL ∙ kg−1 ∙ min−1]” (21). MET-min: An index of energy expenditure that quantifies the total amount of physical activity performed in a standardized manner across individuals and types of activities (21). Calculated as the product of the number of METs associated with one or more physical activities and the number of minutes the activities were performed (i.e., METs × min). It is usually standardized per week or per day as a measure of exercise volume. Kilocalorie (kcal): The energy needed to increase the temperature of 1 kg of water by 1° C. To convert METs to kcal ∙ min−1, it is necessary to know an individual’s body weight. It is usually standardized as kilocalories per week or per day as a measure of exercise volume.
summary: METs, MET-min, and kcal are measures of exercise volume, with METs representing the energy expenditure relative to rest and kcal representing the total energy expended.

content: Progression in exercise depends on an individual’s health status, training response, fitness, and the exercise program goals. Progression allows for improvements in cardiorespiratory fitness while avoiding stagnation in training. Initially, an increase in exercise time/duration per session of 5–10 minutes every 1–2 weeks over the first 4–6 weeks of an exercise training program is reasonable for the average adult. After about 1 month, the frequency, intensity, and time should be gradually increased over the next 4–8 months to meet the recommendations presented in ACSM’s Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription (5). A longer trajectory is recommended for those who are very deconditioned.
summary: Exercise progression should be individualized based on health, fitness, and goals, gradually increasing duration, frequency, and intensity over time.

content: Any progression should avoid making large increases in the FITT components to minimize risks of injury, muscular soreness, or overtraining.
summary: Gradual progression in exercise intensity, time, type, and frequency is essential to minimize injury risk and overtraining.

content: Resistance training is used to optimize muscular fitness including both muscular strength and muscular endurance. Muscular strength refers to the ability of a muscle or muscle group to exert force (e.g., one repetition maximum [1-RM]). Muscular endurance refers to the ability of a muscle or muscle group to continue to perform without fatigue (i.e., repeated contractions or to sustain a contraction). To improve muscular fitness, the muscles must be exposed to an overload (stress beyond the typical activity). This is done via resistance training. Over time, as the muscles adapt to a given overload, the training stimulus must be increased to continue to have gains. This is referred to as progressive overload.
summary: Resistance training enhances both muscular strength and endurance by progressively overloading muscles beyond their typical activity.

content: Details on resistance training programs are found in Chapter 14, and a sample progression is found in Table 13.2. As with cardiorespiratory training programs, the FITT-VP principle can be applied to resistance training. Some clients may have higher level goals in the area of muscular fitness and thus will include an expanded training program.
summary: Resistance training programs should follow the FITT-VP principle, and can be tailored to individual goals, with some clients requiring more extensive training.

content: Over time, as the muscles adapt to a given overload, the training stimulus must be increased to continue to have gains. This is referred to as progressive overload. Details on resistance training programs are found in Chapter 14, and a sample progression is found in Table 13.2. As with cardiorespiratory training programs, the FITT-VP principle can be applied to resistance training.
summary: Progressive overload is essential for continued muscle growth, and resistance training programs should follow the FITT-VP principle.

content: For general muscular fitness, resistance training the major muscle groups (chest, shoulders, back, abdomen, hips, and legs) 2–3 d ∙ wk−1 is recommended. At least 48 hours should separate workouts targeting any given muscle group to allow time for adaptations to occur. Different exercises can be performed on different days. Schedule training days so that at least 48 hours separate training sessions that target the same muscle group.
summary: General muscular fitness recommendations include training major muscle groups 2-3 times per week, with at least 48 hours of rest between sessions targeting the same muscle group.

content: Personal Trainers may incorporate whole-body sessions in which all the major muscle groups are exercised in one session (repeating this sequence a couple times per week) or may train a few selected muscle groups (split program) each session (multiple sessions). In the latter scenario, more frequent resistance training will occur, but individual muscle groups will still only be targeted two to three times per week. A wide variation in program design options can be applied by adopting periods of both low- (1 d ∙ wk−1), medium- (2 d ∙ wk−1) or high-frequency training (>3 d ∙ wk−1) throughout an individualized training plan.
summary: Resistance training programs can be structured as whole-body sessions or split programs, with frequency varying based on individual needs and goals.

content: The time spent at each stage will depend on the client’s muscular fitness level. Transition slowly between the stages (e.g., over time, a beginner can add additional exercises or increase the number of sets to move toward the intermediate level of resistance training).
summary: Resistance training program progression should be tailored to the client's fitness level, gradually increasing intensity and volume over time.

content: Do a total of 10 exercises. Select two exercises from each of these larger muscle group areas: hips and legs, quadriceps, hamstrings, chest, and back. Do a total of five exercises. Select one exercise from each of these smaller muscle group and trunk areas: shoulders, biceps, triceps, low back, and abdominals.
summary: A sample resistance training program includes 10 exercises targeting larger muscle groups and 5 exercises targeting smaller muscle groups.

content: Intensity of resistance training is inversely related to the number of repetitions; with higher resistance, the number of repetitions will be fewer. To improve muscular fitness, typically 8–12 repetitions per set are completed, at an intensity of between 60% and 80% of the client’s 1-RM (i.e., the greatest amount of resistance overcome in a single repetition). For older and very deconditioned individuals, a lower intensity (40%–50% of 1-RM) with 10–20 repetitions is recommended initially. To lower the chance of injury or extreme muscle soreness after exercise, the number of repetitions selected should allow for muscle fatigue at the end of the set but not failure.
summary: Resistance training intensity and repetitions should be tailored to individual fitness levels and goals, typically ranging from 60-80% of 1-RM for 8-12 repetitions.

content: For adults, each muscle group should be trained with two to four sets with rest intervals of 2–3 minutes between sets. Four sets are more effective than two sets, but also realize that for novices, even a single set per exercise will improve muscular strength. When determining the number of sets, attention should be made to adherence and thus may be influenced by the individuals schedule, time availability, and level of commitment.
summary: Resistance training sets and rest intervals should be individualized, with 2-4 sets recommended for adults and consideration given to adherence factors.

content: Resistance training can be done using free weights, machines (stacked weights or pneumatic resistance), rubber bands/cords, body weight, and even alternative and nontraditional implements (kettle bells, medicine balls, battle ropes). For examples of different activities that can be used for the major body areas, see Table 13.3.
summary: Resistance training can be performed using a variety of equipment, including free weights, machines, bodyweight, and unconventional implements.

content: Personal Trainers should include multijoint (e.g., bench press, leg press) as well as potentially single-joint (e.g., biceps curl, quadriceps extension) exercises. When selecting exercises, the Personal Trainer should ensure opposing muscle groups, agonists and antagonists, are included in order to prevent muscle imbalances. For example, including lower back extensions and abdominal crunches to strengthen both the lower back and the abdomen will provide for muscle balance.
summary: Resistance training programs should incorporate both multijoint and single-joint exercises, ensuring balanced muscle development by targeting opposing muscle groups.

content: Dumbbell or band upright row targets the arms (biceps). Machine biceps curl targets the arms (biceps). Dumbbell or band biceps curl targets the arms (biceps). Arms (triceps) Machine biceps press targets the arms (triceps). Dumbbell lying triceps extension targets the arms (triceps).
summary: Examples of exercises targeting the biceps and triceps include dumbbell rows, machine curls, and triceps extensions.

content: Machine back extension targets the low back (erector spinae). Prone plank targets the low back (erector spinae). Kneeling hip extension targets the glutes. Curl-up targets the abdominals. Diagonal curl-up targets the abdominals. Machine abdominal curl targets the abdominals.
summary: Exercises targeting the lower back, glutes, and abdominals include back extensions, planks, hip extensions, and various curl-up variations.

content: Prone plank targets the low back (erector spinae). Kneeling hip extension targets the glutes. Curl-up targets the abdominals. Diagonal curl-up targets the abdominals. Machine abdominal curl targets the abdominals.
summary: This chunk describes exercises targeting specific muscle groups: prone plank for the lower back, kneeling hip extension for the glutes, and curl-ups and machine abdominal curls for the abdominals.

content: Muscular groups should be trained for a minimum of two to four sets. Sets may be the same exercise or from a combination of exercises affecting the same muscle group. For example, either four sets of bench presses or a combination of two sets of bench presses and two sets of dips may be used to train the pectoral muscles.
summary: This chunk explains the recommended training volume for muscle groups, emphasizing that sets can consist of the same exercise or a combination targeting the same muscle.

content: For untrained individuals, lower volume per muscle group per session is needed, whereas for advanced trainees, a greater weekly volume is recommended to promote hypertrophy and strength. Progressive overload in resistance training is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body. One can increase the amount of resistance lifted, increase the number of repetitions, increase the number of sets done per muscle group, or increase the number of days per week the muscle groups are trained.
summary: This chunk discusses training volume recommendations based on experience level and defines progressive overload, outlining various methods for implementing it.

content: If individuals seek to maintain a given level of muscular fitness, it is not necessary to continue to progressively increase the training stimulus. Muscular strength may be maintained by training muscle groups as little as 1 d ∙ wk−1 as long as the training intensity or the resistance lifted is held constant.
summary: This chunk clarifies that maintaining muscular fitness doesn't require continuous progressive overload and outlines the minimum training frequency for maintaining strength.

content: Flexibility exercises have the potential to improve joint range of motion (ROM), physical function, postural stability, and balance. Stretching is recommended as part of a comprehensive training program for adults.
summary: This chunk highlights the benefits of flexibility exercises and recommends their inclusion in adult training programs.

content: Stretching activities should be included a minimum of 2–3 days each week for most adults, although daily flexibility exercise is most effective. Describing the intensity of stretching to a client can be difficult because it is not a measurable entity like a treadmill speed. The Personal Trainer can use cues to help guide clients, such as moving within the ROM to point of mild tightness without discomfort. A stretch should not create discomfort; if so, the client should release slightly. A stretch should never be painful.
summary: This chunk provides recommendations for stretching frequency and offers guidance on communicating intensity to clients, emphasizing the importance of avoiding discomfort or pain during stretching.

content: At least 10 minutes is recommended per session in order to allow all the major muscle–tendon groups to be targeted with at least four repetitions of each stretch. Flexibility can be improved using a wide variety of activities, including static stretching (active and passive), dynamic or slow movement stretching, and proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation. Interestingly, when properly performed, even ballistic or “bouncing” stretches can be as effective as static stretches for increasing joint ROM in individuals engaging in activities that involve ballistic movements such as tennis or basketball.
summary: This chunk outlines recommended stretching duration and variety, noting that even ballistic stretching can be effective for specific activities.

content: Each flexibility exercise per joint should be held at the point of tightness for 10–30 seconds. Time/duration and repetitions of the flexibility exercises should be adjusted to accumulate a total of 60 seconds of stretching at each joint. Recommendations for using proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation are to hold a 20%–75% maximum voluntary contraction for 3–6 seconds, followed by 10–30 seconds of assisted stretch.
summary: This chunk provides specific guidelines for holding stretches and performing proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation.

content: Performing flexibility exercises at least 2–3 d ∙ wk−1 is recommended with daily flexibility exercise being most effective. Neuromotor exercise involves motor skills including balance, coordination, agility, and proprioceptive training. Neuromotor-enhancing activities focus on the communication between feedback from the periphery (e.g., arms and legs) and the interpretation by the central nervous system (e.g., brain and spinal cord).
summary: This chunk reiterates recommended stretching frequency, defines neuromotor exercise, and explains its focus on motor skill development and central nervous system communication.

content: Neuromuscular exercise is recommended at least 2–3 d ∙ wk−1 for 20–30 minutes of duration for the previously mentioned populations. The intensity of balance-related training can be manipulated by Personal Trainers through three aspects: base of support, center of mass, and peripheral cues. Narrowing the base of support increases the challenge of balance training. Displacing the center of mass increases the difficulty of balance training. Peripheral cues, including visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive pathways, can be manipulated to affect the intensity of balance training.
summary: This chunk outlines recommended frequency and duration for neuromotor exercise, specifically focusing on balance training and the methods for manipulating its intensity.

content: The minimum effective dose of balance training has yet to be defined. Improvements in balance have been noted with 20–30 minutes or more per day for a total of 60 min ∙ wk−1. When activities like tai chi are incorporated, typical durations are 45–60 minutes, but the minimum effective time for neuromotor exercise training is not known. Various activities, as well as tai chi, Pilates, and yoga, can be used for balance training.
summary: This chunk discusses the current understanding of the minimum effective dose for balance training, noting that more research is needed and highlighting various activities suitable for balance improvement.

content: Personal Trainers may want to consider advanced training options for new clients with an extensive background in fitness training or for long-term clients who have made great progress since starting their beginner comprehensive fitness programs. A sample beginner adult exercise program is outlined in Table 13.6.
summary: This chunk suggests tailoring training programs based on client experience and provides a reference to a sample beginner adult exercise program.

content: Improvements in balance have been noted with 20–30 minutes or more per day for a total of 60 min ∙ wk−1. Various activities, as well as tai chi, Pilates, and yoga, can be used for balance training. When activities like tai chi are incorporated, typical durations are 45–60 minutes, but the minimum effective time for neuromotor exercise training is not known.
summary: This chunk discusses balance training, noting the benefits of 20-30 minutes daily and mentioning various activities like tai chi, Pilates, and yoga.

content: Personal Trainers may want to consider advanced training options for new clients with an extensive background in fitness training or for long-term clients who have made great progress since starting their beginner comprehensive fitness programs. A sample beginner adult exercise program is outlined in Table 13.6. Week 1–2 of the beginner program includes 3 days per week of aerobic activity for 10–20 minutes per day at light intensity (level 3 or 4). Week 1–2 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with one set of 8–12 repetitions of six exercises. Week 1–2 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. An easy beginning aerobic activity is walking at a comfortable pace. For inactive clients, target 10 minutes at a time for aerobic activity. Include some stretching activities after the walk. For resistance training, see Table 13.3 for details on what activities to include.
summary: This chunk outlines a sample beginner adult exercise program, including details on aerobic activity, resistance training, and stretching for the first two weeks.

content: Week 3–4 of the beginner program includes 3 days per week of aerobic activity for 20–30 minutes per day at light to moderate intensity (level 4 or 5). Week 3–4 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with one or two sets of 8–12 repetitions of six exercises. Week 3–4 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. The focus for the client over the next couple of weeks will be getting comfortable with at least 20 minutes of aerobic exercise at least 3 days per week. Continue with the resistance training program.
summary: This chunk details the beginner exercise program for weeks 3-4, focusing on increasing aerobic activity duration and resistance training sets.

content: Week 5–7 of the beginner program includes 3 or 4 days per week of aerobic activity for 30–40 minutes per day at moderate intensity (level 5). Week 5–7 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with two sets of 8–12 repetitions of six exercises. Week 5–7 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. For the next 3 weeks, the client’s focus is on getting comfortable with up to 40 minutes of aerobic exercise at least 3 days per week (for each week, add 5–10 minutes per session). Continue with the resistance training program, completing two sets per exercise and adding more weight if the 12 repetitions for a given exercise now feel easy.
summary: This chunk outlines the beginner exercise program for weeks 5-7, emphasizing further increases in aerobic activity duration and resistance training intensity.

content: Week 8–10 of the beginner program includes 3 or 4 days per week of aerobic activity for 35–50 minutes per day at moderate intensity (level 5 or 6). Week 8–10 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with two sets of 8–12 repetitions of six exercises. Week 8–10 of the beginner program includes 2 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. Over the past couple of months, the client will develop a good aerobic fitness base. For some variety, other activities such as biking or swimming can be included (for more ideas, see Chapter 15). If the client likes walking, that is also an option.
summary: This chunk details the beginner exercise program for weeks 8-10, focusing on continued increases in aerobic activity duration and suggesting alternative activities for variety.

content: For your resistance training program, consider adding some variety and trying some other exercises (see Table 13.3 for ideas). All activity sessions should be preceded and followed by a 5- to 10-minute warm-up and cool-down. Include stretching activities after aerobic exercise to improve flexibility. For specific stretches to target the major muscle groups, see Chapter 16. Resistance training is more fully outlined in Chapter 14. Beginners should select one exercise for each of the following body areas (see Table 13.3): hips and legs, chest, back, shoulders, low back, and abdominals.
summary: This chunk provides general advice on resistance training, warm-ups, cool-downs, stretching, and exercise selection for beginners.

content: A sample intermediate-level adult exercise program is outlined in Table 13.7. Week 1–2 of the intermediate program includes 3 or 4 days per week of aerobic activity for 35–50 minutes per day at moderate intensity (level 5 or 6). Week 1–2 of the intermediate program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with one or two sets of 8–12 repetitions of 8–10 different exercises. Week 1–2 of the intermediate program includes 2 or 3 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. Aerobic activity is included for a total of 150–200 minutes per week (moderate-intensity activity). For resistance training, include exercises for biceps and triceps (in addition to the body areas previously targeted) and add exercises for the quadriceps and hamstrings in the second week, so by the end of this stage, the client will include a total of 10 exercises (see Table 13.3).
summary: This chunk outlines the first two weeks of a sample intermediate-level adult exercise program, detailing aerobic activity, resistance training, and stretching.

content: Week 3–5 of the intermediate program includes 3–5 days per week of aerobic activity for 30–60 minutes per day at moderate intensity (levels 5–7). Week 3–5 of the intermediate program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with one or two sets of 8–12 repetitions of 10 different exercises.
summary: This chunk details the intermediate exercise program for weeks 3-5, focusing on increased aerobic activity duration and resistance training exercises.

content: Week 1–2 of the intermediate program includes 2 or 3 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. For resistance training, include exercises for biceps and triceps (in addition to the body areas previously targeted) and add exercises for the quadriceps and hamstrings in the second week, so by the end of this stage, the client will include a total of 10 exercises (see Table 13.3).
summary: Weeks 1-2 of the intermediate program focus on stretching and gradually increasing resistance training exercises to target 10 muscle groups.

content: Week 3–5 of the intermediate program includes 3–5 days per week of aerobic activity for 30–60 minutes per day at moderate intensity (levels 5–7). Week 3–5 of the intermediate program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with one or two sets of 8–12 repetitions of 10 different exercises. Week 3–5 of the intermediate program includes 2 or 3 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. The focus for the next 3 weeks is to increase the time spent in aerobic exercise or to increase the intensity (don’t do both at the same time). If the client feels more comfortable with moderate-intensity activity, 200 minutes per week is appropriate. If the client is ready to increase intensity (e.g., jogging rather than walking), cut back on the time to 20–30 minutes per day (note that the target for vigorous-intensity activity is 75–100 minutes per week). A mix of moderate- and vigorous-intensity activity is also an option (see Chapter 15 for more details). Continue with the resistance training program.
summary: Weeks 3-5 of the intermediate program emphasize increasing aerobic activity duration and intensity, maintaining resistance training, and continuing stretching.

content: Week 6–10 of the intermediate program includes 3–5 days per week of aerobic activity for 30–50 minutes per day at moderate intensity (level 6). Week 6–10 of the intermediate program includes 2 days per week of resistance training with two sets of 8–12 repetitions of 10 exercises. Week 6–10 of the intermediate program includes 2 or 3 days per week of 10 minutes of stretching activities. For the client’s aerobic activity, either increase the time spent per day or increase the number of days per week. Ultimately, the weekly total should be 200–300 minutes of moderate-intensity activity or 100–150 minutes of vigorous-intensity activity (recall that 2 minutes of moderate activity equals 1 minute of vigorous activity) or a combination of moderate and vigorous activity.
summary: Weeks 6-10 of the intermediate program continue to increase aerobic activity duration and intensity, maintain resistance training, and continue stretching, aiming for a total of 200-300 minutes of moderate-intensity or 100-150 minutes of vigorous-intensity activity per week.

content: The sample established adult exercise program recommends 5 days per week of moderate exercise, 3 days per week of vigorous exercise, or 3–5 days per week of a mix of moderate and vigorous exercise. The program recommends 2 days per week of resistance training with two sets of 8–12 reps of 10 different exercises. The program recommends a minimum of 3 days per week of stretching activities for 10 minutes. The target for aerobic activity is 200–300 minutes of moderate-intensity activity or 100–150 minutes of vigorous-intensity activity. Resistance training exercise examples are found in Table 13.3. For weeks 3–4, the program recommends 2 or 3 days per week of moderate activity and 1 or 2 days per week of vigorous activity. For weeks 5–6, the program recommends 5 days per week of moderate exercise, 3 days per week of vigorous exercise, or 3–5 days per week of moderate and vigorous exercise. For weeks 7–8, the program recommends 5 days per week of moderate exercise, 2 days per week of vigorous exercise, or 3–5 days per week of moderate and vigorous exercise. All activity sessions should be preceded and followed by a 5- to 10-minute warm-up and cool-down. Include stretching activities after aerobic exercise to improve flexibility. Resistance training is more fully outlined in Chapter 14. Select one exercise for each of the following body areas: hips and legs, chest, back, shoulders, low back, abdominals, quadriceps, hamstrings, biceps, and triceps.
summary: This program outlines a sample exercise plan for adults, recommending moderate and vigorous aerobic activity, resistance training, and stretching, with specific recommendations for each week.

content: Speed refers to the ability to perform a movement within a short amount of time. Agility refers to the ability to change the position of the body in space with speed and accuracy. Coordination refers to the ability to use the senses, such as sight and hearing, together with body parts in performing tasks smoothly and accurately. Balance refers to the maintenance of equilibrium while stationary or moving. Power refers to the rate at which one can perform work. Reaction time refers to the time elapsed between stimulation and the beginning of the response. These skill-related components can be achieved simultaneously through several types of advanced training options such as HIIT, high-velocity weight training, Olympic weightlifting, plyometric training, balance training, sport- and nonsport-specific agility, and coordination drills. Sprint drills are an example of activities involving the speed skill component.
summary: This chunk defines and lists various skill-related components of fitness, including speed, agility, coordination, balance, power, and reaction time, and provides examples of training methods to improve them.

content: Balance refers to the maintenance of equilibrium while stationary or moving. Yoga/Pilates, Balance Stability exercises (traditional exercises performed on an uneven surface such as a foam pad or BOSU ball), and Yoga/Pilates are exercise types. Flexibility exercises have been shown to increase balance and reaction time (1).
summary: Balance is the ability to maintain equilibrium, and exercises like Yoga, Pilates, and balance stability training can improve it.

content: Power refers to the rate at which one can perform work. Plyometric exercises link strength with speed of movement to produce power and were first known simply as ‘jump training’. Plyometric exercises begin with a quick stretch of the muscle fibers (the eccentric phase) and then followed by a fast shortening of the same muscle fibers (the concentric phase) (1). Power can be increased through resistance training and by performing repetitions of lower loads at a high velocity (22).
summary: Power is the rate of work performed, and plyometric exercises, which involve rapid muscle contractions, are effective for developing it.

content: Reaction time refers to the time elapsed between stimulation and the beginning of the response. Sprint drills are an example of activities involving the speed skill component. There are many exercises and drills that Personal Trainers can use to condition their clients that require them to jump, run, slide, and bound in nontraditional movement patterns that will develop their coordination, reaction time, and balance skills. All three of these skills involve muscle activation along with sensory integration to perform exercise-related tasks in a more highly skilled way.
summary: Reaction time is the delay between stimulus and response, and exercises involving speed, agility, and coordination can improve it.

content: These skill-related components can be achieved simultaneously through several types of advanced training options such as HIIT, high-velocity weight training, Olympic weightlifting, plyometric training, balance training, sport- and nonsport-specific agility, and coordination drills. Power resistance training can improve a client’s ability to increase speed of movement and power production as well as increase agility levels. Both high-velocity resistance training and Olympic weight training yield increases in power production.
summary: Advanced training methods like HIIT, Olympic weightlifting, and plyometrics can improve multiple skill-related components simultaneously.

content: Balance, coordination, and reaction time are needed for recreational purposes but also used in daily routines.
summary: Balance, coordination, and reaction time are essential for both recreational activities and daily life.

content: The components of an exercise training session include the warm-up, conditioning exercise, cool-down, and stretching. The warm-up is, at a minimum, 5–10 minutes of low to moderate activity intended to literally warm-up the muscles in preparation for the conditioning phase. The warm-up provides opportunity for the body to adjust from resting status to the higher physiological, biomechanical, and bioenergetic demands of the conditioning phase (5). Warm-up activities include cardiovascular and muscular endurance activities. The conditioning phase is the main focus of the exercise session and may include one or more of the following: cardiorespiratory (aerobic) exercise, resistance training, neuromotor activities, and sport-specific activities.
summary: A complete exercise session includes a warm-up, conditioning phase, cool-down, and stretching.

content: The cool-down involves similar cardiovascular and muscular endurance activities as found in the warm-up, but now, the transition is from the higher intensity of the conditioning phase back toward resting status. During this time of low- to moderate-intensity activity, the body will experience a decrease in heart rate and systolic blood pressure, and metabolic end products (e.g., lactate) produced during more intense activity will be removed. Allowing for a gradual return toward baseline will help the client avoid postexercise hypotension (low blood pressure) and potential dizziness (due to blood pooling in the legs rather than flowing back to the heart and brain). The cool-down should be at least 5–10 minutes, longer for higher intensity conditioning phase sessions (5).
summary: The cool-down phase gradually reduces exercise intensity, allowing the body to recover and avoid post-exercise hypotension.

content: The stretching phase is considered distinct from the warm-up and cool-down phases (5). For adults in a general fitness program and for athletes in sports in which flexibility is important, stretching after the warm-up is typically recommended (5). Stretching can also be included after the cool-down. For some sports (e.g., those focused on muscular strength, power, and endurance), some research suggests stretching following the activity rather than following the warm-up (5). When properly performed, even ballistic or ‘bouncing’ stretches can be as effective as static stretches for increasing joint ROM in individuals engaging in activities that involve ballistic movements such as tennis or basketball (5).
summary: Stretching, distinct from warm-up and cool-down, is beneficial for flexibility and can be incorporated before, after, or even during exercise depending on the sport or activity.

content: A complete fitness program provides many physiological and psychological benefits. Garber CE, Blissmer B, Deschenes MR, et al. published American College of Sports Medicine position stand. Quantity and quality of exercise for developing and maintaining cardiorespiratory, musculoskeletal, and neuromotor fitness in apparently healthy adults: guidance for prescribing exercise. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2011;43(7):1334–59. Chodzko-Zajko WJ, Proctor DN, Fiatarone Singh MA, et al. published American College of Sports Medicine position stand. Exercise and physical activity for older adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2009;41(7):1510–30. Available from: https://health.gov/sites/default/files/2019-09/Physical_Activity_Guidelines_2nd_edition.pdf. summary: Regular exercise offers numerous physiological and psychological benefits, and guidelines emphasize the importance of physical activity for all ages and health conditions.

content: Owen N, Healy GN, Matthews CE, and Dunstan DW published Too much sitting: the population health science of sedentary behavior. Exerc Sport Sci Rev. 2010;38(3):105–13. Blair SN published Physical inactivity: the biggest public health problem of the 21st century. Br J Sports Med. 2009;43(1):1–2.  Bergen G, Stevens MR, and Burns ER published Falls and fall injuries among adults aged ≥65 years — United States, 2014. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep. 2016;65(37):993–8.

Kohrt WM, Bloomfield SA, Little KD, Nelson ME, and Yingling VR published American College of Sports Medicine position stand: physical activity and bone health. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2004;36(11):1985–96. Ratamess NA, Alvar BA, Evetoch TK, Housh TJ, Kibler WB, and Kraemer WJ published American College of Sports Medicine position stand. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2009;41(3):687–708.
summary: This chunk features publications from the American College of Sports Medicine, including guidelines for exercise testing and prescription, a position stand on physical activity and bone health, and a position stand on progression models in resistance training.

J Strength Cond Res. 2019;33(8):2019–52. Westcott WL published Resistance training is medicine: effects of strength training on health. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2012;11(4):209–16.

Poole L and Steptoe A published Depressive symptoms predict incident chronic disease burden 10 years later: findings from the English Longitudinal Study of Ageing (ELSA). J Psychosom Res. 2018;113:30–6. Schuch FB, Vancampfort D, Richards J, et al. published Exercise as a treatment for depression: a meta-analysis adjusting for publication bias. J Psychiatr Res. 2016;77:42–51. Depress Anxiety. 2019;36(1):39–53.

content: Contrada RJ and Baum A edited The Handbook of Stress Science: Biology, Psychology, and Health. New York (NY): Springer; 2011. 708 p.
summary: This chunk highlights a comprehensive handbook on stress science, covering its biological, psychological, and health aspects.

content: Gheysen F, Poppe L, DeSmet A, et al. published Physical activity to improve cognition in older adults: can physical activity programs enriched with cognitive challenges enhance the effects? A systematic review and meta-analysis. Int J Behav Nutr Phys Act. 2018;15(1):63. Barnes JN published Exercise, cognitive function, and aging. Adv Physiol Educ. 2015;39(2):55–62.
summary: This chunk explores the relationship between physical activity, cognitive function, and aging, examining the potential benefits of enriched physical activity programs for older adults.

content: Resistance training, also known as strength training or weight training, is a standard part of a comprehensive Personal Training program. A resistance training program can affect almost every system in the body and is used in a wide variety of populations. The benefits of resistance training are numerous and include increases in strength, muscle mass, and bone density. Almost every population can benefit from resistance training, from children preparing for youth sports to individuals trying to counteract the effects of the aging process.
summary: This chunk provides a general overview of resistance training, its benefits, and its applicability across diverse populations.

content: At the end of the Second World War, Captain Thomas DeLorme, MD, experimented with the use of progressive resistance exercise as a rehabilitation modality for injured soldiers. A few years later, DeLorme and A. L. Watkins published the first paper in a scientific journal on the topic of long-term resistance training. The most influential personalities in resistance training were Mr. Bob Hoffman of York Barbell Club, who pioneered the interest in Olympic-style weightlifting and weight training with free weights through his publications and sales of barbells and dumbbells, and Mr. Joe Weider and his brother Ben, who promoted the bodybuilding industry.
summary: This chunk traces the historical development of resistance training, highlighting key figures and milestones.

content: The science of resistance training did not pick up again until two notable former weightlifters and future scientists, Dr. Patrick O’Shea from Oregon State University and Dr. Richard Berger from Temple University, fueled an explosion of scientific work in the 1960s and 1970s. Since then, published research on resistance training has grown exponentially with a gradual widening in research focus from enhancing athletic performance to also improving health and fitness.
summary: This chunk describes the resurgence of scientific interest in resistance training and its expanding scope beyond athletic performance.

content: Although resistance training programs are critical for athletic and sports performance, they have now become a foundation of a variety of rehabilitation disciplines from orthopedic to cardiac to obesity management programs. Research on resistance training now appears in many medical and scientific journals such as the American College of Sports Medicine’s (ACSM) Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise and the National Strength and Conditioning Association’s Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. Thousands of scientific articles examining various aspects of resistance training with regard to designing optimal exercise prescriptions across the population spectrum are in print.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the widespread application of resistance training in various fields, including rehabilitation and its prominent presence in scientific literature.

content: The terms resistance exercise and resistance training are often used interchangeably; however, there is an important distinction between the two terms. Resistance exercise refers to a single exercise session, whereas resistance training refers to a planned, structured program of resistance exercises performed over time. Resistance training refers to the combination of many consecutive resistance exercise sessions over time.
summary: This chunk clarifies the distinction between resistance exercise and resistance training, emphasizing the importance of structured programming.

content: Designing a resistance training program is a very individualized process, and the needs and goals of the client are paramount to the selection of program characteristics. Although a client may be training to maximize muscle hypertrophy, the client will also develop some muscular strength and endurance. The general principles of any effective resistance training program are as follows: Specificity of training: Only the muscles that are trained will adapt and change in response to a given program. For this reason, resistance programs must target all muscles for which a training effect is desired. The SAID principle indicates that the adaptation will be specific to the demands that the exercise places on the individual.
summary: This chunk outlines the principles of designing individualized resistance training programs, emphasizing specificity and the SAID principle.

content: Resistance exercise refers to a single exercise session, whereas resistance training refers to the combination of many consecutive resistance exercise sessions over time.
summary: This chunk reiterates the distinction between resistance exercise and resistance training.

content: Resistance exercise refers to a single exercise session, whereas resistance training refers to the combination of many consecutive resistance exercise sessions over time.
summary: This chunk reiterates the distinction between resistance exercise and resistance training.

content: Resistance exercise refers to a single exercise session, whereas resistance training refers to the combination of many consecutive resistance exercise sessions over time. Designing a resistance training program is a very individualized process, and the needs and goals of the client are paramount to the selection of program characteristics. Although a client may be training to maximize muscle hypertrophy, the client will also develop some muscular strength and endurance.
summary: Resistance exercise vs. training, and the individualized nature of program design based on client goals.

content: The general principles of any effective resistance training program are as follows: Specificity of training: Only the muscles that are trained will adapt and change in response to a given program. For this reason, resistance programs must target all muscles for which a training effect is desired. The SAID principle indicates that the adaptation will be specific to the demands that the exercise places on the individual.
summary: Key principles of resistance training: specificity and SAID principle.

content: As the body adapts to a given stimulus, an increase in the stimulus is required for further adaptations and improvements. No one program should be used without changing the exercise stimulus over time. Periodization is the phasic manipulation of the training variables (volume, intensity, frequency, and rest intervals) as a means of optimizing desired physiological outcomes while concurrently reducing the incidence of overtraining. Periodization allows for optimal training and recovery time in a resistance training program.
summary: Progressive overload, the need for program variation, and the benefits of periodization.

content: It is difficult to train for all aspects of muscular fitness. Within a periodized training program, one needs to focus or prioritize the training goals for each training cycle. The key to a great program design is to identify the specific variables that need to be controlled to best predict and/or ensure the desired training outcomes.
summary: Limitations of training, the importance of prioritization within periodization, and program design considerations.

content: The most challenging aspect of resistance training exercise prescription is deciding which changes in program design will best meet an individual’s training goals. Appropriate changes in the resistance training program are required over time. Sound decisions must take into consideration the needs of the sport or activity, the individual training response, and available testing data. The resistance training exercise prescription is a continual process of planning, assessing, and changing.
summary: Challenges of program design, the need for ongoing adjustments, and the importance of client-specific considerations.

content: Planning a resistance training exercise prescription allows one to quantify the exercise stimulus. Planning ranges from the initial development of a single exercise session to the variation of the training program over time. A successful Personal Trainer needs the ability to quantify a client’s workout and evaluate progress made to ensure the program is safe and effective and will lead to optimal physical development of the client.
summary: The role of planning in resistance training, from individual sessions to program variation, and the importance of program evaluation.

content: The gains made in any variable related to muscular performance will ultimately be linked to an individual’s genetic potential. A deconditioned individual will likely achieve large initial gains because of the great adaptive potential available. As training proceeds, the rate of adaptation slows, as an individual approaches genetic limits. Over time, the physiological adaptation as measured from baseline will continue to increase albeit at a much slower rate.
summary: The influence of genetics on training adaptations, initial gains vs. long-term progress.

content: At this point in the training process, because gains are less noticeable, other goals for the resistance training program must be targeted to prevent the client from losing interest because of a lack of progress. Appreciation of the process by which adaptations occur over time is critical in developing an optimal program.
summary: Addressing plateaus and the importance of understanding the long-term nature of training adaptations.

content: When working with a new client, Personal Trainers should always devote adequate time to evaluate the clients’ prior resistance exercise experience and discuss their training goals carefully before beginning any exercise sessions. A needs analysis should focus on learning about the client’s personal goals and needs. The intended time frame for achieving these goals should be considered. Targeted areas or muscle groups should be identified. Health issues such as cardiovascular disease, asthma, diabetes, osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, immune system disorders, neurological disorders, musculoskeletal limitations, recent surgeries, chronic injuries, and sites of pain should be assessed.
summary: Importance of client assessment, needs analysis, and consideration of health factors.

content: Personal Trainers should try to understand the motivation underlying their clients’ goals. Personal Trainers should assess the level of support their clients feel they have from family or friends. Personal Trainers should discuss any prior resistance training experience their clients have had. Personal Trainers work with clients to develop strategies to overcome potential barriers to resistance training.
summary: Understanding client motivation, support systems, and addressing potential barriers to training.

content: Initial assessments will help Personal Trainers determine which muscle groups, energy systems, and muscle actions need to be trained. Before developing a resistance training program, Personal Trainers should conduct a baseline fitness assessment. The baseline fitness assessment should consist of anthropometric measurements (height, weight, circumferences, skinfolds, etc.), resting hemodynamics (heart rate, blood pressure), body composition, and tests of muscular strength and endurance.
summary: Initial assessments and the importance of a baseline fitness evaluation.

content: Muscular strength assessments include the one repetition maximum (1-RM) testing on a variety of exercises, especially those exercises that involve the major muscle groups such as bench press and squat, but only if tolerable to the client. The 1-RM test allows the Personal Trainer to determine loading values for a particular exercise. There are specific procedures that should be followed when conducting a 1-RM test to help ensure safety, reliability, and validity of the test.
summary: Baseline muscular strength assessment using 1-RM testing and considerations for safety and validity.

content: Before developing a resistance training program, Personal Trainers should conduct a baseline fitness assessment. The baseline fitness assessment should consist of anthropometric measurements (height, weight, circumferences, skinfolds, etc.), resting hemodynamics (heart rate, blood pressure), body composition, and tests of muscular strength and endurance.
summary: A baseline fitness assessment is crucial before creating a resistance training program, encompassing anthropometrics, hemodynamics, body composition, and muscular strength and endurance tests.

content: Muscular strength assessments include the one repetition maximum (1-RM) testing on a variety of exercises, especially those exercises that involve the major muscle groups such as bench press and squat, but only if tolerable to the client. The 1-RM test allows the Personal Trainer to determine loading values for a particular exercise. There are specific procedures that should be followed when conducting a 1-RM test to help ensure safety, reliability, and validity of the test.
summary: Muscular strength assessments, particularly 1-RM testing, are essential for determining loading values and require specific procedures to ensure safety and accuracy.

content: Although 1-RM is a measure of maximal strength, muscular power can be tested by using the medicine ball “put” for the upper body power assessment and the vertical jump test or standing long jump for the lower body muscular power assessment. Muscular endurance testing might include curl-ups, push-ups, or maximal amount of repetitions that can be performed at a given percentage of the 1-RM load.
summary: Beyond maximal strength, muscular power and endurance can be assessed using various tests like medicine ball throws, jumps, and repetitions at specific percentages of 1-RM.

content: It is important that Personal Trainers keep records of their clients’ progress. Individualized training logs are a useful tool for monitoring progress. Training logs should record specific exercises, resistance or load, number of sets, and number of repetitions. Another possible measure to consider on the training log is a rating of perceived exertion (RPE) similar to what was discussed in Chapter 15. However, there is a resistance training–specific RPE, termed the OMNI-Resistance Exercise Scale (OMNI-RES). Clients rank the last repetition of each set using a 0–10 scale (0 being extremely easy and 10 being extremely hard). The training log provides a record of the resistance and knowledge of performance of previous exercise sessions, which is necessary for assigning the appropriate resistance for successive sessions. Kept over time, these logs provide the Personal Trainer with a means to examine and evaluate program effectiveness or to identify areas of weakness.
