A traditional linear periodization program contains four phases: Hypertrophy phase, consisting of high volume and short rest periods. Strength/power phase, consisting of reduced volume but increased load and rest periods. Peaking phase, consisting of low volume but high load and longer rest periods. Recovery phase, consisting of low volume and load. There is no set formula for how a program should be periodized because it depends on the specific goals and needs of the client.
summary: Periodization involves systematically varying training volume and intensity over time. A typical linear periodization program includes hypertrophy, strength/power, peaking, and recovery phases, tailored to individual goals.

content: A traditional linear periodization program contains four phases: Hypertrophy phase, consisting of high volume and short rest periods. Strength/power phase, consisting of reduced volume but increased load and rest periods. Peaking phase, consisting of low volume but high load and longer rest periods. Recovery phase, consisting of low volume and load.
summary: Linear periodization involves four phases: hypertrophy, strength/power, peaking, and recovery, each with specific volume and intensity targets.

content: Periodization acts as a way to systematically vary the workout over time. Incorporating periodization into the training program systematically varies the acute program variables, which exposes muscles to different stimuli, leading to greater muscular adaptation and performance. Rest is encouraged at different points in the training program, which allows for appropriate recovery and the prevention of both short- and long-term overtraining. Another important benefit to periodization is that it can reduce the potential boredom found with repeating the same resistance exercise program over and over again, which may improve program adherence.
summary: Periodization systematically varies training variables to optimize adaptation, recovery, and adherence.

content: There is no set formula for how a program should be periodized because it depends on the specific goals and needs of the client. Table 14.4 presents an example of a traditional four-phase periodized training program aimed at producing maximal strength. The use of periodized resistance training has been shown to be superior to constant training methods. Periodized training involves the planned variation in the intensity of exercises and in the volume of a workout. Typically, one periodized large muscle group exercises. However, periodization schemes can be created for smaller muscle groups as well.
summary: Periodization is individualized and has been shown to be more effective than constant training.

content: Many different models for periodization have been developed. The terms micro-, meso-, and macrocycle refer to the time course of the different phases of periodization. The macrocycle is the largest training cycle time frame. A common example of a macrocycle is a calendar year, and all phases are included in this cycle. A mesocycle refers to the next smaller group of training cycles that make up the macrocycle, usually four to six in a year. The microcycle is the smallest component, which usually ranges in time from 1 to 4 weeks and is typically dedicated to one type of workout variable in that phase (e.g., high-volume, low-intensity power).
summary: Periodization is structured using macrocycles, mesocycles, and microcycles, each with specific durations and training focuses.

content: A primary theory suggests that a greater variation in the training stimulus will produce greater overall adaptations in the body. There are two basic types that have been developed for maximal strength development: linear and nonlinear periodized protocols. Classic periodization methods use a progressive increase in the intensity with small variations in each 1- to 4-week microcycle. An example of a classic 16-week, four-cycle linear periodized program is presented in Table 14.5. Although there are some variations within each microcycle, the general trend for the 16-week program is a steady linear increase in the intensity of the training program. Microcycle 5 is a 2-week active rest period in which no lifting is done or only a very light, low-volume training is used prior to the next mesocycle. Because of the straight-line increase in the intensity of the program, it has been termed “linear” periodized training. Linear periodization originally evolved from training for single-peak performance events (e.g., track and field, weightlifting). Thus, consecutive or linear buildup in the training intensity to the peak was used.
summary: Linear periodization involves a gradual increase in intensity over time, often used for single-peak performance goals.

content: The volume of the training program in the classic periodization program will gradually decrease in concert with an increase in intensity. The volume–intensity trade-off can be lessened as an individual improves training status. In other words, advanced athletes can tolerate higher volumes of exercise during heavy and very heavy microcycles. A meta-analysis of the research has shown that progressing to an average of eight sets per muscle group is the optimal dose needed to stimulate muscular strength adaptations in muscular strength in advanced lifters as compared to an average of three sets per muscle group for beginners and four sets for intermediate lifters.
summary: Volume and intensity are strategically balanced in linear periodization, with adjustments based on training experience.

content: One must be very careful not to train with high volumes and heavy weights too quickly; monitor the stress of the workouts and the total conditioning program. Pushing too hard has the potential for creating a serious overtraining syndrome. Overtraining can compromise progress for weeks or even months. Although it takes a great deal of excessive work to produce this type of overtraining effect, highly motivated individuals can easily make these mistakes out of sheer desire to make gains and see rapid progress in their training.
summary: Careful monitoring and pacing are crucial to avoid overtraining, which can hinder progress.

content: The idea of high-volume exercise in the early microcycles is that it may promote the muscle hypertrophy needed to eventually enhance strength in the Later phases of training are dependent on the early phases of training. Programs that attempt to gain strength without developing the needed hypertrophy of muscle tissue are limited in their potential.
summary: Early phases of training often focus on hypertrophy to support later strength gains.

content: Overtraining can compromise progress for weeks or even months. Although it takes a great deal of excessive work to produce this type of overtraining effect, highly motivated individuals can easily make these mistakes out of sheer desire to make gains and see rapid progress in their training.
summary: Overtraining can hinder progress, even for highly motivated individuals who may push themselves too hard.

content: The idea of high-volume exercise in the early microcycles is that it may promote the muscle hypertrophy needed to eventually enhance strength in the. Later phases of training are dependent on the early phases of training. Programs that attempt to gain strength without developing the needed hypertrophy of muscle tissue are limited in their potential.
summary: Early high-volume training promotes muscle hypertrophy, which is essential for later strength gains.

content: Increases in the intensity of a periodized program allow for the development of the needed nervous system adaptations for enhanced motor unit recruitment. As a program progresses, heavier weights require higher threshold motor units to become involved in the force production process. The subsequent increase in muscle protein from the early cycle training enhances force production from the motor units.
summary: Periodized training progressively increases intensity, leading to improved nervous system adaptations and enhanced force production.

content: One mesocycle is the completion of all of the cycles in a 16-week program. A year training program (macrocycle) is made up of several mesocycles. Multiple and short mesocycles allow for delineating different trainable features of muscle. In theory, each mesocycle can progress the body’s musculature upward toward one’s genetic limitations.
summary: Training programs are structured into mesocycles and macrocycles, allowing for targeted muscle development and progression towards genetic potential.

content: The theoretical basis for a linear method of periodization consists of developing the body with a sequential loading from light to heavy and from high volume to low volume, thereby addressing the goals of the program for that training cycle while providing active rest at the completion of the mesocycle. This is repeated again and again with each mesocycle, and progress is made in the training program over an entire macrocycle.
summary: Linear periodization involves a gradual progression from light to heavy loads and high to low volume, allowing for targeted training and recovery.

content: Reverse linear periodization is a technique that follows the tenants of linear periodization for volume and strength; however, it is in the reverse order. One study has shown that reverse linear periodization is beneficial when muscular endurance is the primary program outcome.
summary: Reverse linear periodization prioritizes muscular endurance by starting with heavier weights and lower volume, gradually progressing to lighter weights and higher volume.

content: The concept of nonlinear periodized training programs (also called daily undulating periodization or DUP) has been developed to maintain variation in the training stimulus. Nonlinear periodized training enhances program implementation because it is flexible and can accommodate schedule, business, or competitive demands placed on the individual. The nonlinear program allows variation in the intensity and volume within each week over the course of the training program (e.g., 12 wk). Active rest is then taken after the 12-week mesocycle.
summary: Nonlinear periodization, or DUP, involves daily variations in intensity and volume, offering flexibility and adaptability to individual schedules.

content: Recent research has shown that nonlinear periodized training can have similar or greater beneficial effects on resistance training outcomes when compared with traditional linear periodization. Rhea and colleagues (86) showed the nonlinear periodization training elicited a greater percentage of strength gains as compared with linear training. Prestes and colleagues (90) found that undulating periodization induces greater increases in maximal strength as compared with linear periodization. These studies suggest that daily variations in the undulation training had a superior effect on maximizing strength as compared with weekly or monthly variation.
summary: Research supports the effectiveness of nonlinear periodization, demonstrating comparable or superior strength gains compared to linear periodization.

content: One of the theorized reasons for the success of nonlinear periodization is that unlike linear programs, the different components of muscle size, strength, and power are trained with the goal of attempting to train different features of muscle within the same time frame (e.g., hypertrophy and power and strength). Thus, an individual stimulates multiple physiological adaptations within the same 7- to 10-day period of the 12-week mesocycle.
summary: Nonlinear periodization simultaneously targets different muscle adaptations (size, strength, power) within a shorter timeframe, potentially leading to greater overall gains.

content: Often, busy travel, school, or competition schedules conflict with time requirements of traditional training program. A DUP program is highly adaptable to variations in a client’s schedule. This type of training model may enhance adherence as it may “fit” the client’s schedule better than the traditional linear method. Additionally, constantly altering program variables keeps the program interesting and challenging for clients, thereby reducing the boredom of repeating the same program.
summary: DUP programs offer flexibility and adaptability to busy schedules, potentially improving adherence and client engagement.

content: Theoretically, a DUP program can be different with each and every training session. Because the workouts are not linear, the different workouts change with different training sessions. If the Monday workout is missed, with DUP, that workout is performed on the next training day and the sequence is continued. In this way, no workout stimulus is missed in the training program. With a DUP type program, any mesocycle will be completed when a certain number of workouts are completed (e.g., 48) rather than counting the completion of a set number of weeks. With a DUP type program, any mesocycle will be completed when a certain number of workouts are completed (e.g., 48) rather than counting the completion of a set number of weeks.
summary: DUP programs allow for flexibility in scheduling, ensuring all workout stimuli are incorporated and mesocycles are completed based on workout frequency rather than time.

content: One of the new advances in periodization is called ‘unplanned nonlinear periodization.’ The name is somewhat of a misnomer because an overall plan is developed for a 12-week mesocycle, but the actual day that a given workout will be performed is based on the readiness to train. Thus, it is also termed ‘flexible nonlinear periodization.’
summary: Flexible nonlinear periodization allows for adjustments in workout scheduling based on individual readiness, while still adhering to a planned mesocycle structure.

content: With a DUP-type program, any mesocycle will be completed when a certain number of workouts are completed (e.g., 48) rather than counting the completion of a set number of weeks. With a DUP type program, any mesocycle will be completed when a certain number of workouts are completed (e.g., 48) rather than counting the completion of a set number of weeks.
summary: DUP programs structure mesocycles based on workout completion rather than a fixed timeframe.

content: One of the new advances in periodization is called ‘unplanned nonlinear periodization.’ The name is somewhat of a misnomer because an overall plan is developed for a 12-week mesocycle, but the actual day that a given workout will be performed is based on the readiness to train. Thus, it is also termed ‘flexible nonlinear periodization.’
summary: Unplanned nonlinear periodization involves a pre-planned 12-week mesocycle, but workout selection is flexible based on the client's readiness.

content: In unplanned or flexible nonlinear periodization, a workout plan is set for the mesocycle, but deciding what workout is to be done on what day is left to the Personal Trainer, who will base it on the client’s fatigue level, psychological state, or fitness, to use only the most optimal workout that can be performed on a given day. In this model, the training session category (e.g., light, moderate, power, or heavy) is prescribed on the basis of the physiological ability or state of the client at the time of the session. Thus, if the client is very fatigued before a particular exercise session, some workouts would not be prescribed. For example, a power training or plyometrics training day or a high-volume, low-rest training day would not be a good choice because fatigue would reduce the workout quality. After a specific workout is completed, it is checked off in the major planning matrix for the 12-week mesocycle. Again, the goal for any mesocycle in this type of nonlinear periodization program is to complete each planned workout rather than “x” number of weeks.
summary: Flexible nonlinear periodization allows for workout adjustments based on the client's daily readiness, prioritizing optimal training sessions.

content: In any periodization model, it is the primary exercises that are typically periodized, but one can also use a two-cycle periodization program to vary the small muscle group exercises. For example, in the “triceps push-down,” one could rotate between the moderate (8–10 RM) and the heavy (4–6 RM) cycle intensities. This would provide not only the hypertrophy needed for such isolated muscles of a joint but also the strength needed to support heavier workouts of the large muscle groups.
summary: Periodization can be applied to both primary and smaller muscle group exercises, optimizing both hypertrophy and strength development.

content: Different approaches can be used to periodization a resistance training program. Programs can be linear, reverse linear, nonlinear ‘daily undulating,’ or unplanned/flexible nonlinear schedules. Periodized programs are more effective than constant-intensity training programs for increasing strength. Effective periodization is accomplished by melding specific program goals (strength, hypertrophy, muscular endurance, or some combination) with appropriate variations in volume–intensity and frequency of training. The key to workout success is variation, and different periodization approaches can be used to accomplish this training need.
summary: Various periodization models exist, with periodized programs generally proving more effective for strength gains than constant-intensity training.

content: The level of fitness and resistance training experience of the client is perhaps the most important factor to be considered when designing a resistance training program. Resistance exercise can place a large stress on the body, and certain exercises require high levels of skill to avoid injury. Thus, exercise technique is the most important aspect of resistance training for beginners. At the beginning of the training program, correct technique should be significantly emphasized, and the resistance and volume should be kept low.
summary: Client fitness level and experience are crucial considerations when designing a resistance training program, with emphasis on proper technique for beginners.

content: Resistance exercise can place a large stress on the body, and certain exercises require high levels of skill to avoid injury. From a short-term performance-enhancement point of view, a single set per exercise may be enough for beginners to achieve the stimulus needed from an exercise. However, depending on the individual, multiple sets, even for beginners may be beneficial (9,10,32,75). Some studies have found that multiple sets even for beginners create larger improvements than single sets, whereas no study has found that single sets are superior (10,31). One reason for this is that more repetitions can lead to faster improvements in exercise technique, especially for multijoint exercises.
summary: While single sets may suffice for beginners, multiple sets can be beneficial, potentially leading to faster technique improvements.

content: The squat exercise is an exercise that requires a great deal of technique to be performed correctly. Only doing one set of a few squats does not allow the client many practice trials of this complex movement. There is a dose-response relationship with progressive resistance training. As a person becomes accustomed to a given stimulus or dose of exercise, additional stress is needed to elicit the given response or increase in strength, muscular endurance, hypertrophy, power, or all four. As the client progresses past the initial few months of training, multiple sets should be used for each exercise session.
summary: The squat demands proper technique, and multiple sets are crucial for progress as the body adapts to training stimuli.

content: As the skill and experience level of the client improves, more technical exercises can be taught. Advanced resistance training can include highly technical exercises such as the clean or the snatch as well as advanced modalities such as plyometric exercises. The progression of advanced exercises will differ among individuals. The Personal Trainer must evaluate each client extensively and continuously before including more advanced exercises to ensure that the exercises match the client’s skill and experience level. Clients hire Personal Trainers for a variety of reasons.
summary: Advanced training programs incorporate complex exercises and modalities, requiring careful progression based on individual client capabilities.

content: Clients hire Personal Trainers for a variety of reasons. Many clients hire Personal Trainers because they feel they need constant guidance. In addition, Personal Trainers provide them with a support system. Most importantly, clients desire to hire professionals with training and knowledge in conditioning science. Clients want a professional to help them perform exercises properly and who understands exercise prescription to allow them to achieve their personal goals and objectives. For some clients, it is an important part of their sports conditioning program.
summary: Clients seek Personal Trainers for guidance, support, expertise, and personalized training plans to achieve their fitness goals.

content: The Personal Trainer must form a special relationship with each and every client that is based on professionalism, trust, and openness. Clients should feel that their Personal Trainer genuinely cares about them and is personally vested in helping them achieve their goals. Clients expect their Personal Trainer to be a source of knowledge and an educator. Clients expect their Personal Trainer to be able to explain things or answer the question “why?” Clients appreciate having their Personal Trainer explain the reason they are doing a particular exercise or combination of sets and reps in their program.
summary: A successful Personal Trainer-client relationship is built on trust, open communication, and the Personal Trainer acting as a knowledgeable guide and educator.

content: Personal Training has been found to be superior to unsupervised training, even for people who understand resistance training. Personal Trainers should convey the specific benefits of resistance training, including increases in strength, muscle mass, and bone mass, particularly to clients who may be skeptical about why resistance training is important. Some uneducated clients may have false impressions of the outcomes from resistance training or on how to go about attaining optimal gains. Some men may do too much of one exercise (e.g., biceps curls) trying to get huge, thereby creating muscular imbalance or women may hold back from performing any heavy loading because of the “fear of getting big muscles.” These mistakes may at best diminish the gains that could be realized with resistance training and at worse result in serious overtraining or acute injury.
summary: Personal training offers advantages over unsupervised training, and Personal Trainers should educate clients on the benefits of resistance training while addressing common misconceptions and potential pitfalls.

content: Clients consider Personal Trainers experts and will often want to hear their opinion on fads facing the fitness industry. Often, clients’ knowledge of resistance training comes from infomercials or magazine advertisements. Personal Trainers need to stay up-to-date with the scientific literature in order to provide accurate information and current research to their clients. Personal Trainers should develop a network of experts who can act as resources for when clients ask questions that the Personal Trainer does not know the answer. It is always best to admit you do not know the answer than to convey potentially incorrect information. Because Personal Trainers are required to obtain continuing education credits to maintain their certifications, staying current is critical to success.
summary: Personal Trainers should stay informed about fitness trends and research, be transparent about their knowledge, and build a network of resources to provide accurate information to clients.

content: A key aspect in Personal Training is the ability to demonstrate a given lift or technique. Providing the client with a good visual representation of the lift will allow the client to then replicate the movement pattern. The Personal Trainer should demonstrate each lift with proper form with a verbal explanation of the lift or technique and then actually physically demonstrate proper form of the given lift. Resistance training often requires that the Personal Trainer have physical contact with the client to ensure correct positioning, fit and setup of a machine, and proper technique in both machine and free-weight exercises. Personal Trainers should take time to explain to clients the spotting procedures in resistance training and the level of physical interaction required. Always ask your clients before physically touching them to ensure that they are comfortable with it.
summary: Effective Personal Training involves clear demonstrations, verbal explanations, and physical guidance to ensure proper technique and client comfort.

content: For example, when performing elbow extension exercises, it is sometimes helpful for the Personal Trainer to place his or her hands on the client’s elbows as a reminder to keep the elbow from pointing outward. In these cases, explain to the client, ‘I am going to put my hands on your elbows to remind you to keep them from pointing outward. Is this okay with you?’ Good spotting technique is vital for a safe resistance training program. Personal Trainers must understand the movement technique of every exercise and how to position clients to get the proper anatomical positioning throughout the exercises. In addition, Personal Trainers need to know where to position themselves to spot appropriately for each exercise. The goal of correct spotting is to prevent injury.
summary: Spotting techniques are crucial for safety, and Personal Trainers must be knowledgeable about proper positioning and spotting procedures for each exercise.

content: A lifter should always have an exercise spotted, and the Personal Trainer must mediate this process, alone or with additional help. A checklist for the Personal Trainer is the following: Know proper exercise technique, Know proper spotting technique, Be sure you are strong enough to assist the lifter with the resistance being used or get help, Know how many repetitions the lifter intends to do, Be attentive to the lifter at all times, Stop lifters if exercise technique is incorrect or they break form, Know the plan of action if a serious injury occurs.
summary: Personal Trainers are responsible for ensuring safe spotting practices, including knowing proper technique, being physically capable, and having a plan in case of injury.

content: As the skill and experience level of the client improves, more technical exercises can be taught. Advanced resistance training can include highly technical exercises such as the clean or the snatch as well as advanced modalities such as plyometric exercises. The progression of advanced exercises will differ among individuals. The Personal Trainer must evaluate each client extensively and continuously before including more advanced exercises to ensure that the exercises match the client’s skill and experience level.
summary: Progression to advanced exercises should be individualized and based on the client's skill level and experience.

content: Personal Trainers must understand the movement technique of every exercise and how to position clients to get the proper anatomical positioning throughout the exercises. In addition, Personal Trainers need to know where to position themselves to spot appropriately for each exercise. The goal of correct spotting is to prevent injury. A lifter should always have an exercise spotted, and the Personal Trainer must mediate this process, alone or with additional help. A checklist for the Personal Trainer is the following: Know proper exercise technique, Know proper spotting technique, Be sure you are strong enough to assist the lifter with the resistance being used or get help, Know how many repetitions the lifter intends to do, Be attentive to the lifter at all times, Stop lifters if exercise technique is incorrect or they break form, Know the plan of action if a serious injury occurs.
summary: This chunk outlines the essential responsibilities of a Personal Trainer, emphasizing proper exercise technique, spotting, and injury prevention.

content: Each program should be designed on the basis of the principles outlined in this chapter. Periodization is very important, and many Personal Trainers are now using nonlinear methods to keep the clients interested and the programs effective (93).
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of program design principles, particularly periodization, for effective training.

content: A large number of resistance exercises can be used in a program. The reader is referred to a comprehensive list of more than 125 exercise descriptions of both machine and free-weight exercises along with spotting techniques by Kraemer and Fleck (93).
summary: This chunk mentions the variety of exercises available for programming and refers to a resource for further exercise information.

content: Free weights and machines can be used for each exercise as well as bilateral and unilateral exercises.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the flexibility in exercise selection, including the use of free weights, machines, and unilateral/bilateral variations.

content: See Figure 14.5 for examples of 15 basic exercises: (A) squat, (B) supine leg press, (C) 45° leg press, (D) lunge, (E) leg extensions, (F) leg curls, (G) machine vertical bench press, (H) smith supine bench press, (I) free-weight supine bench press, (J) dumbbell bench press, (K) machine seated rows, (L) front lat pull-down, (M) dumbbell arm curls, (N) barbell arm curls, and (O) triceps push-down.
summary: This chunk lists 15 basic exercises, providing examples for various muscle groups and equipment types.

content: Leg curls target the hamstrings and can be performed bilaterally or unilaterally. To perform leg curls, lie face down on a machine, grab the handles, and lift the lever arm by flexing the knees until they are straight. Leg curls can be performed in standing, seated, or lying positions.
summary: This chunk describes leg curls, their target muscle group, and variations in execution.

content: The vertical machine bench press targets the chest and triceps. To perform the vertical machine bench press, sit on the seat with the line of the grips just below the chest and the bar line an inch above the chest. Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and make sure the feet are flat on the ground. Push the lever arm straight out until the elbows are straight to complete a repetition.
summary: This chunk details the vertical machine bench press, its target muscles, and the correct execution technique.

content: The Smith supine bench press targets the chest and triceps. To perform the Smith supine bench press, lie flat on the bench with the upper chest under the bar, place the feet flat on the floor, and keep the shoulders and hips on the bench at all times. Grasp the bar with elbows at 45° angles, disengage the bar hooks from the Smith machine, lower the weight to the chest, and press the bar up until the arms are extended. Rehook the bar to the machine when completed.
summary: This chunk describes the Smith supine bench press, its target muscles, and the correct execution technique.

content: The free-weight supine bench press targets the chest and triceps. To perform the free-weight supine bench press, lie flat on the bench with the upper chest under the bar, place the feet flat on the floor, and keep the shoulders and hips on the bench at all times. Grasp the bar with elbows at 45° angles, lower the weight to the chest, and press the bar up until the arms are extended. Rerack the bar with a spotter’s help when completed.
summary: This chunk details the free-weight supine bench press, its target muscles, and the correct execution technique.

content: The dumbbell bench press targets the chest, upper arms, and triceps. To perform the dumbbell bench press, start in a seated position on the bench with a dumbbell in each hand resting on the lower thigh. Lift the weights to the shoulder, lie back on the bench, position the dumbbells to the side of the upper chest, press the dumbbells up until the arms are extended, and return to the starting position. Return to the seated position with the dumbbells on your thighs or have the spotter take the dumbbells when completed. Two spotters may be necessary if heavy weights are used.
summary: This chunk describes the dumbbell bench press, its target muscles, and the correct execution technique.

content: Machine seated rows target the upper back. To perform machine seated rows, take a seated position with the chest against the pad, grasp the lever vertical handles with a vertical or horizontal overhand grip, and pull the lever back until the elbows are in line with the upper body. Check the seat height so that the chest is directly in front of the lever handles and check whether the client is pulling in a straight line parallel to the ground. The client can use an overhand grip as a variation to the movement, using the other horizontal handles.
summary: This chunk describes the machine seated rows, its target muscle group, and the correct execution technique.

content: The front lat pull-down targets the upper back. To perform the front lat pull-down, use a locked grip, grasp the cable bar with a wide grip, sit with thighs under machine support, and pull the bar down to the upper chest. Return to the starting position to complete the repetition.
summary: This chunk describes the front lat pull-down, its target muscle group, and the correct execution technique.

content: Dumbbell arm curls target the upper arm and biceps. To perform dumbbell arm curls, take a seated position with two dumbbells held at the sides, with the palms facing in and the arms hanging straight down.
summary: This chunk describes dumbbell arm curls, their target muscle group, and the starting position.

content: The front lat pull-down targets the upper back. To perform the front lat pull-down, use a locked grip, grasp the cable bar with a wide grip, sit with thighs under machine support, and pull the bar down to the upper chest. Return to the starting position to complete the repetition.
summary: Instructions and target muscle group for the front lat pull-down exercise.

content: Dumbbell arm curls target the upper arm and biceps. To perform dumbbell arm curls, take a seated position with two dumbbells held at the sides, with the palms facing in and the arms hanging straight down. Raise the dumbbells and rotate the forearm so that the palms face the shoulder, lower to the original position to complete one repetition, and one can also alternate one arm at a time.
summary: Instructions and target muscle group for dumbbell arm curls.

content: Barbell arm curls target the upper arm and biceps. To perform barbell arm curls, stand in the standing position with the feet shoulder-width apart, grasp the straight barbell with an underhand grip and palms facing up, raise the bar until the forearms are vertical, and then lower the bar to the starting position to complete a repetition. One can also perform this exercise with an e-z bar with the palms facing inward.
summary: Instructions and target muscle group for barbell arm curls, including variations.

content: Triceps push-downs target the upper arm and triceps. Position the hands above the bar prior to the push-down phase of the repetition. Other types of attachments also can be used (e.g., rope, v-bar).
summary: Instructions and target muscle group for triceps push-downs, mentioning attachment variations.

content: Development of a resistance training program is a systematic process in which science and art come together to allow the Personal Trainer to specifically address a client’s needs for neuromuscular fitness. A sequence of events in the exercise prescription process consists of getting a client’s medical clearance, Personal Training history, goal generation, a needs analysis, and a general preparation phase of initial training and testing before putting together workouts based on the acute program variables that will be used in a resistance training program. This program is then updated and revised with the same process over time.
summary: Describes the systematic process of developing a resistance training program, emphasizing client-specific needs and ongoing program adjustments.

content: Education, client interactions, and motivation are vital components of successful resistance training programs that meet each client’s goals and objectives.
summary: Highlights the importance of education, client engagement, and motivation for successful resistance training programs.

content: Paoli A, Gentil P, Moro T, Marcolin G, and Bianco A published a study in Front Physiol in 2017. Schoenfeld BJ published a study in Sports Med in 2013. Schoenfeld BJ, Grgic J, Ogborn D, and Krieger JW published a systematic review and meta-analysis in J Strength Cond Res in 2017. Grgic J, Schoenfeld B, Skrepnik M, Davies T, and Mikulic P published a systematic review in Sports Med in 2017. content: The American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) position stand (1) states that the scientific evidence demonstrating the beneficial effects of exercise is indisputable and the benefits of exercise far outweigh the risks in most adults. Regular cardiorespiratory, resistance, flexibility, and neuromotor exercise training is considered 'essential for most adults' according to the ACSM (1).
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of exercise for health and well-being, highlighting the ACSM's stance on its benefits.

content: In 1953, more than 55% of U.S. children failed to meet a minimum standard of muscular fitness and health compared with about 8% for European children. Expert panel meetings were held in the 1960s and 1970s to summarize research on the benefits of different exercise training regimens for improving cardiovascular fitness. Michael Pollock's 1973 article (3) served as the basis of the first ACSM position statement entitled 'Quantity and Quality of Exercise for Developing and Maintaining Fitness in Healthy Adults' (4).
summary: This chunk provides historical context about the evolution of exercise recommendations and the ACSM's early position statements.

content: The first ACSM position statement recommended performing moderate to vigorous cardiorespiratory exercise using large muscle groups for 15–60 minutes for 3–5 days per week to improve cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF). As research expanded, it became apparent that significant health-related benefits could be achieved at lower levels of physical activity. In 1990, a distinction was made between physical activity recommendations for health and physical activity recommendations for fitness.
summary: This chunk details the initial ACSM recommendations for cardiorespiratory fitness and the subsequent refinement of guidelines based on new research.

content: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the ACSM (5) combined recommendations with the 1996 Surgeon General’s guidelines (6). The CDC/ACSM recommendations suggest accumulating at least 30 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity on most days of the week. Previous guidelines recommended continuous exercise of at least 20 minutes in duration. In 1998, ACSM updated its position stand to include muscular fitness and flexibility components in addition to cardiorespiratory recommendations.
summary: This chunk outlines the evolution of physical activity recommendations, incorporating guidelines from various sources and expanding to include muscular fitness and flexibility.

content: The 2018 Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, second edition, state that adults should move more and sit less throughout the day. Some physical activity is better than none. Adults who sit less and do any amount of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity gain some health benefits. The 2018 Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, second edition, recommend performing moderate- to vigorous-intensity exercise throughout the week and that additional health benefits can be gained by performing at least 300 minutes of moderate-intensity exercise.
summary: This chunk presents the latest physical activity guidelines, emphasizing the importance of regular movement, even small amounts, and the benefits of moderate-to-vigorous exercise.

content: For substantial health benefits, adults need to accumulate at least 150 minutes (2 h and 30 min) per week of moderate-intensity physical activity or 75 minutes (1 h and 15 min) per week of vigorous-intensity physical activity. The ACSM position stand includes over 400 references providing an extensive summary of the scientific evidence and most up-to-date recommendations for professionals concerning individualized exercise prescription. The ACSM position stand recommends ≥5 days per week of moderate exercise, ≥3 days per week of vigorous exercise, or a combination of moderate and vigorous exercise on ≥3–5 days per week. Moderate- and/or vigorous-intensity exercise is recommended for most adults. Light- to moderate-intensity exercise may be beneficial in deconditioned persons.
summary: This chunk provides specific recommendations for weekly exercise volume and intensity, highlighting the ACSM's comprehensive approach to exercise prescription.

content: For substantial health benefits, adults need to accumulate at least 150 minutes (2 h and 30 min) per week of moderate-intensity physical activity or 75 minutes (1 h and 15 min) per week of vigorous-intensity physical activity. 30–60 minutes per day (150 minutes per week) of purposeful moderate exercise, 20–60 minutes per day (75 minutes per week) of vigorous exercise, or a combination of moderate and vigorous exercise per day is recommended for most adults. Less than 20 minutes per day (<150 minutes per week) of exercise can be beneficial in previously sedentary persons.
summary: Recommended weekly duration and frequency of moderate and vigorous exercise for adults, with considerations for previously sedentary individuals.

content: The ACSM position stand includes over 400 references providing an extensive summary of the scientific evidence and most up-to-date recommendations for professionals concerning individualized exercise prescription. The ACSM position stand recommends ≥5 days per week of moderate exercise, ≥3 days per week of vigorous exercise, or a combination of moderate and vigorous exercise on ≥3–5 days per week. Regular, purposeful exercise that involves major muscle groups and is continuous and rhythmic in nature is recommended. ≥500–1,000 MET ∙ min ∙ wk is recommended.
summary: ACSM position stand recommendations for exercise prescription, frequency, and type.

content: One continuous session per day or in multiple ≥10-minute sessions to accumulate the desired duration and volume of exercise per day is recommended. Less than 10 minutes per session may yield favorable adaptation in very deconditioned individuals. Gradual progression of exercise volume by adjusting exercise duration, frequency, and/or intensity until the desired exercise goal (maintenance) is attained is recommended.
summary: Guidance on structuring exercise sessions and progressing exercise volume over time.

content: CRF refers to the ability of an individual to perform large muscle, rhythmic, repetitive, and continuous moderate- to vigorous-intensity exercise for an extended period of time. The goal of CRF training is to increase heart rate (HR) and respiration in order to place an appropriate physiological stress on the cardiorespiratory system. The increase in stress during CRF training is often referred to as ‘overload.’ Overload of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems is required to create beneficial adaptations in CRF.
summary: Definition and principles of cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) training, emphasizing the role of overload.

content: CRF is a function of enhancing both the central oxygen delivery (i.e., heart and circulatory) processes and the peripheral oxygen-uptake mechanisms of the working muscles. Enhancing the body’s ability to deliver and use oxygen for metabolic processes allows one to do more work. Typical measurements used to determine improvements in CRF include increases in maximal volume of oxygen consumed per unit time (V̇ O2max) and decreases in HR or V̇ O2 in response to a given submaximal workload.
summary: How CRF is developed, focusing on central and peripheral adaptations, and common metrics for assessing improvement.

content: Major benefits of increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity include an overall decrease in morbidity and mortality. Increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity decrease the risk of premature death from coronary artery disease. Increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity decrease the incidence of cardiovascular disease, stroke, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, metabolic syndrome, osteoporotic fractures, and gallbladder disease.
summary: Health benefits associated with increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity, emphasizing reduced risk of chronic diseases.

content: Increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity lower the risks of developing cancers of the bladder, colon, endometrium, esophagus, kidney, lung, and stomach. Increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity decrease the risk of dementia (including Alzheimer disease), anxiety, and depression. Increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity increase the likelihood of increased habitual activity levels that are also associated with health benefits.
summary: Additional health benefits of increased cardiorespiratory training and physical activity, including reduced cancer risk and improved mental health.

content: Improvement in Cardiovascular and Respiratory Function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes increased maximal oxygen uptake resulting from both central and peripheral adaptations. Improvement in Cardiovascular and Respiratory Function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes decreased minute ventilation at a given absolute submaximal intensity. Improvement in Cardiovascular and Respiratory Function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes decreased myocardial oxygen cost for a given absolute submaximal intensity. Improvement in Cardiovascular and Respiratory Function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes decreased heart rate and blood pressure at a given submaximal intensity.
summary: Specific physiological adaptations in cardiovascular and respiratory function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training.

content: Improvement in Cardiovascular and Respiratory Function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes increased capillary density in skeletal muscle. Improvement in Cardiovascular and Respiratory Function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes increased exercise threshold for the accumulation of lactate in the blood. Improvement in Cardiovascular and Respiratory Function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes increased exercise threshold for the onset of disease signs or symptoms (e.g., angina pectoris, ischemic ST-segment depression, claudication).
summary: Further physiological adaptations in cardiovascular and respiratory function resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training, including improved lactate threshold and exercise tolerance.

content: Reduction in Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes reduced resting systolic/diastolic pressures. Reduction in Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes increased serum high-density lipoprotein cholesterol and decreased serum triglycerides. Reduction in Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes reduced total body fat and reduced intraabdominal fat. Reduction in Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes reduced insulin needs and improved glucose tolerance. Reduction in Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes reduced blood platelet adhesiveness and aggregation.
summary: Cardiovascular risk factor improvements associated with increased cardiorespiratory training.

content: Decreased Morbidity and Mortality resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes higher activity and/or fitness levels are associated with lower death rates from coronary artery disease. Decreased Morbidity and Mortality resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes higher activity and/or fitness levels are associated with lower incidence rates for combined cardiovascular diseases, coronary artery disease, stroke, Type 2 diabetes, osteoporotic fractures, and gallbladder disease. Decreased Morbidity and Mortality resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes lower risks of cancers of the bladder, breast, colon, endometrium, esophagus, kidney, lung and stomach.
summary: Long-term health outcomes and reduced mortality associated with increased cardiorespiratory training.

content: Reduction in Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes reduced insulin needs and improved glucose tolerance. Reduction in Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes reduced blood platelet adhesiveness and aggregation.
summary: Increased cardiorespiratory training reduces coronary artery disease risk factors, including improved glucose metabolism and reduced blood clotting.

content: Decreased Morbidity and Mortality resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes higher activity and/or fitness levels are associated with lower death rates from coronary artery disease. Decreased Morbidity and Mortality resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes higher activity and/or fitness levels are associated with lower incidence rates for combined cardiovascular diseases, coronary artery disease, stroke, Type 2 diabetes, osteoporotic fractures, and gallbladder disease. Decreased Morbidity and Mortality resulting from increased cardiorespiratory training includes lower risks of cancers of the bladder, breast, colon, endometrium, esophagus, kidney, lung and stomach.
summary: Increased cardiorespiratory training reduces morbidity and mortality from various diseases, including cardiovascular diseases, cancer, and diabetes.

content: Regular physical activity/exercise interventions act as secondary prevention (i.e., interventions after a cardiac event help prevent another). Based on meta-analyses (pooled data across studies), cardiovascular and all-cause mortality are reduced in postmyocardial infarction patients who
summary: Regular exercise acts as secondary prevention after cardiac events, reducing mortality risk.

content: Randomized controlled trials of cardiac rehabilitation exercise training involving postmyocardial infarction patients do not support a reduction in the rate of nonfatal reinfarction. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may decrease anxiety and depression. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may decrease the risk of dementia, including Alzheimer disease. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may enhance physical function and independent living in older persons. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may enhance feelings of well-being. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may enhance performance of work, recreational, and sport activities. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may reduce the risk of falls and injuries from falls in older persons. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may prevent or mitigate functional limitations in older adults. Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training may be an effective therapy for many chronic diseases in older adults.
summary: Cardiac rehabilitation exercise training offers various benefits beyond reducing mortality, including improved mental health, cognitive function, physical abilities, and overall well-being.

content: Performing activities that increase heart rate and respiration are required to overload or challenge the cardiorespiratory system. Altering the mode or type of exercise brings about specific adaptations as well as more generalized cardiorespiratory fitness gains.
summary: Cardiorespiratory training requires activities that elevate heart rate and respiration, and different exercise types lead to specific and general fitness adaptations.

content: The minimal amount of overload needed to bring about the desired adaptation is referred to as the ‘threshold’ for change. If the training level exceeds the threshold, then characteristic physiological adaptations occur. Exceeding the threshold that is required for physiological adaptations to occur can result in diminished performance.
summary: Training overload must exceed a threshold to induce adaptations, but exceeding this threshold can lead to diminished performance.

content: Overtraining syndrome is characterized by the chronic accumulation of too much training, leading to long-term diminished performance and taking weeks to overcome. The principle of diminishing returns describes the theoretical ‘genetic ceiling’ that each individual has in regard to his or her ability to continually improve his or her fitness with training.
summary: Overtraining syndrome results from excessive training, while the principle of diminishing returns highlights the individual limits to fitness improvement.

content: As an individual reaches his or her genetic ceiling, the amount and rate of improvement that occurs slows and eventually plateaus. If a client is injured or decreases his or her exercise below the threshold of change, the principle of reversibility will apply, where physiological effects and health benefits will decrease with the reduction in exercise.
summary: Fitness gains plateau as individuals approach their genetic potential, and reduced exercise leads to a decline in fitness.

content: The FITT principles of exercise prescription include frequency, intensity, time, and type. A well-planned cardiorespiratory training (CRF) session should include a warm-up, a conditioning phase, and a cool-down.
summary: Exercise prescription follows the FITT principles, and a well-structured session includes warm-up, conditioning, and cool-down phases.

content: The warm-up prepares the person for the conditioning phase, where a target intensity is to be achieved allowing for appropriate overload. The cool-down allows the person to transition back toward resting levels.
summary: The warm-up prepares the body for exercise, the conditioning phase provides the targeted training, and the cool-down facilitates recovery.

content: A properly constructed exercise program will include a transition period from rest to the target exercise intensity called the warm-up. The duration, intensity, and type of exercises performed during the warm-up will vary depending on the individual and purpose of the warm-up.
summary: A warm-up is a gradual transition from rest to the target exercise intensity, tailored to the individual and exercise goals.

content: During the warm-up, the client should perform low-level activities similar to what will be done during the conditioning phase. An appropriate warm-up for a brisk walking exercise program would include slow walking. The warm-up may also include gentle dynamic stretching activities, although stretching should not be done with cold muscles.
summary: Warm-up activities should resemble the target exercise and include dynamic stretching, avoiding static stretching with cold muscles.

content: The specific activities included in a warm-up will vary depending on the target activity to be included in the endurance phase. In general, the warm-up should include 5–10 minutes of low-intensity large muscle activity that progresses to an intensity at the lower end of the target exercise range for the endurance phase.
summary: Warm-up content is tailored to the target exercise, typically involving 5-10 minutes of low-intensity activity progressing to the lower end of the target intensity.

content: The intent of a warm-up is to prepare the muscles and cardiorespiratory system for the upcoming workout. The warm-up is a time of transition and should provide a gradual (rather than an abrupt) increase in heart rate (HR), respiration, and body temperature.
summary: The warm-up prepares the body for exercise by gradually increasing heart rate, respiration, and body temperature.

content: Taking sufficient time to prepare the body for physical activity increases the safety and enjoyment of the target exercise during the endurance phase. The benefits of completing a warm-up include reducing the susceptibility of injury to muscles or joints by increasing the extensibility of connective tissue.
summary: A proper warm-up enhances exercise safety and enjoyment by preparing the body and reducing injury risk.

content: The warm-up should include 5–10 minutes of low-intensity large muscle activity that progresses to an intensity at the lower end of the target exercise range for the endurance phase. The intent of a warm-up is to prepare the muscles and cardiorespiratory system for the upcoming workout. The warm-up is a time of transition and should provide a gradual (rather than an abrupt) increase in heart rate (HR), respiration, and body temperature. Taking sufficient time to prepare the body for physical activity increases the safety and enjoyment of the target exercise during the endurance phase.
summary: A warm-up gradually increases heart rate, respiration, and body temperature, preparing the body for exercise and reducing injury risk.

content: The benefits of completing a warm-up include reducing the susceptibility of injury to muscles or joints by increasing the extensibility of connective tissue. The benefits of completing a warm-up include improving joint range of motion and function. The benefits of completing a warm-up include improving muscle performance. The benefits of completing a warm-up include helping prevent ischemia (lack of oxygen) of the heart muscle, which may occur in clients with sudden strenuous exertion. The warm-up allows a transition from rest to the conditioning phase, which is at a higher level of intensity.
summary: Warm-ups offer numerous benefits, including injury reduction, improved joint function, enhanced muscle performance, and cardiovascular protection.

content: The cool-down is a transition from the higher intensity of the conditioning phase back toward resting levels. The cool-down reduces the risk of cardiovascular complications by allowing HR, blood pressure, and respiration rate to gradually decrease toward resting levels. By allowing a gradual progression toward resting rather than abruptly stopping exercise, the client will avoid an acute, excessive drop in blood pressure that could result in dizziness. A gradual decrease in intensity also helps remove metabolic end products (e.g., lactate) from muscles used more intensely during the conditioning phase.
summary: The cool-down gradually reduces heart rate, blood pressure, and respiration, minimizing cardiovascular strain and aiding in metabolic recovery.

content: Furthermore, 5–10 minutes of static stretching that incorporates all the major muscle groups should be incorporated following the aerobic cool-down. Stretching may help reduce the potential risks of muscle cramping. Approximately 10 minutes of diminishing intensity activities are appropriate for a typical cool-down. For exercise done at higher intensity during the conditioning phase, a longer cool-down may be warranted. For the client who uses brisk walking as an exercise mode for the endurance phase, an appropriate cool-down could be a bit shorter and could include slow walking for 5 minutes followed by 5 minutes of dynamic body stretches.
summary: The cool-down should include static stretching and a gradual decrease in intensity, with duration adjusted based on exercise intensity.

content: According to the ACSM guidelines, the optimal frequency of aerobic exercise appears to be 3–5 d ∙ wk−1 for most adults, with the frequency varying with the intensity. The 2018 Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans suggest that adults should accumulate 150 min ∙ wk−1 of moderate-intensity exercise. These guidelines recommend that adults should do any amount of moderate to vigorous physical activity at least 3 d ∙ wk−1. Although additional benefits may be achieved beyond 5 d ∙ wk−1, improvements can be attenuated as the frequency increases such that a plateau in benefits is often seen with exercise done greater than 5 d ∙ wk−1. Training at high intensity or vigorous activity for greater than 5 d ∙ wk−1 might increase the incidence of injury and is not recommended for most adults. Individuals focused on competition or high-level performance will likely train 6 or more days a week for specific physiological adaptations.
summary: Recommended aerobic exercise frequency is 3-5 days per week for most adults, with intensity and duration influencing frequency.  Higher frequency may not provide additional benefits beyond 5 days per week.

content: There is an inverse relationship between the recommendations for frequency and intensity such that if the exercise intensity is held at the lower end of the target range, then the frequency can be increased and vice versa. Current evidence suggests that multiple combinations of frequency and duration can be used to meet current physical activity recommendations. For example, 5 d ∙ wk−1 of moderate-intensity exercise for 30 minutes may not be any different than performing 3 d ∙ wk−1 of moderate-intensity exercise for 50 minutes. In addition, performing aerobic exercise only once or twice a week at a moderate- to vigorous-intensity can still confer health benefits for a client.
summary: Exercise frequency and intensity can be adjusted to meet individual needs and goals, with various combinations achieving the same health benefits.

content: A client’s initial level of fitness is one variable to consider when determining the appropriate exercise intensity needed to achieve the required overload. The principle of initial values suggests that intensity levels are necessarily higher in fit than in unfit individuals because the threshold for cardiorespiratory benefits is higher. The Personal Trainer must understand that the minimum intensity threshold for improvement varies based on initial fitness level, age, health status, physiological differences, genetics, habitual physical activity, and social and psychological factors.
summary: Exercise intensity should be individualized based on a client's fitness level, age, health, and other factors to ensure an appropriate training stimulus.

content: For individuals with lower fitness, intensity levels as low as 45% oxygen uptake reserve (V̇ O2R) and heart rate reserve (HRR) may provide a sufficient challenge to increase V̇ O2max. In contrast, highly trained athletes may train at 95%–100% V̇ O2max. Moderately trained individuals may find that 70%–80% V̇ O2max provides a sufficient training stimulus. Recent evidence suggests that performing vigorous-intensity exercise (>6 metabolic equivalents [METs], 60%–84% HRR) is more effective at increasing V̇ O2max than moderate-intensity (3.0–5.9 METs, 40%–59% HRR) exercise, but moderate- to vigorous-intensity exercise is best used to meet current physical activity recommendations.
summary: Optimal exercise intensity varies based on fitness level, with lower intensity for beginners and higher intensity for trained individuals. Vigorous-intensity exercise is most effective for increasing VO2max, but moderate-intensity exercise is recommended for general health.

content: Vigorous exercise intensity is rated 60–89 on the Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) scale, corresponding to 6.0–8.7 on a scale of 0 to 10. Near maximal to maximal exercise intensity is rated ≥90 on the RPE scale, corresponding to ≥8.8 on a scale of 0 to 10. Very hard exercise intensity is rated ≥96 on the RPE scale, corresponding to ≥18 on a scale of 0 to 10.
summary: Exercise intensity can be subjectively rated using the RPE scale, with higher ratings indicating greater intensity.

content: Interval training is broadly defined as performing intermittent bouts of vigorous- to supramaximal-intensity exercise (20–240 s) separated by equal or longer bouts of light- to moderate-intensity exercise (60–340 s).
summary: Interval training involves alternating periods of high-intensity exercise with periods of lower-intensity recovery.

content: Recent evidence suggests that performing vigorous-intensity exercise (>6 metabolic equivalents [METs], 60%–84% HRR) is more effective at increasing V̇ O2max than moderate-intensity (3.0–5.9 METs, 40%–59% HRR) exercise, but moderate- to vigorous-intensity exercise is best used to meet current physical activity recommendations. For moderate- to vigorous-intensity exercise, the intensity range is 40%–89% HRR.
summary: Vigorous exercise is more effective for increasing V̇ O2max, but moderate-to-vigorous intensity is recommended for general physical activity.

content: Vigorous exercise intensity is rated 60–89 on the Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) scale, corresponding to 6.0–8.7 on a scale of 0 to 10. Near maximal to maximal exercise intensity is rated ≥90 on the RPE scale, corresponding to ≥8.8 on a scale of 0 to 10. Very hard exercise intensity is rated ≥96 on the RPE scale, corresponding to ≥18 on a scale of 0 to 10.
summary: Exercise intensity is measured using the RPE scale, with vigorous, near maximal, and very hard intensities having distinct RPE ratings.

content: Interval training is broadly defined as performing intermittent bouts of vigorous- to supramaximal-intensity exercise (20–240 s) separated by equal or longer bouts of light- to moderate-intensity exercise (60–340 s). High-intensity interval training (HIIT) or sprint-interval training is a type of interval training. The 2018 Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee found that HIIT can improve cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) in adults with varied weight and health status. HIIT can improve insulin sensitivity and blood pressure to a greater extent in overweight or obese adults. Interval training can be aerobic-based, resistance-based, or a combination of the two.
summary: Interval training involves alternating bouts of high-intensity and low-intensity exercise, with HIIT being a specific type. It improves CRF and metabolic health.

content: The type of interval training program, intensity, exercise interval duration, recovery intervals, and total number of intervals performed are based on the goals of the training session and the physical readiness of the client.
summary: Interval training program parameters are individualized based on client goals and fitness level.

content: There are many methods for determining exercise intensity. Some methods for determining exercise intensity require knowledge of measured V̇ O2max, maximal heart rate (HRmax), and/or resting heart rate (HRrest). Other methods for determining exercise intensity rely on estimations of HRmax based on age.
summary: Exercise intensity can be determined using various methods, including measured physiological data and age-based estimations.

content: Personal Trainers must use the information available to determine the most appropriate exercise prescription. A good Personal Trainer has the willingness and ability to modify the exercise program to provide the appropriate overload and prescription to the client.
summary: Personal trainers should tailor exercise prescriptions based on client information and adjust them as needed.

content: Using heart rate (HR) in prescribing intensity can be helpful because it represents a client’s physiological response. Accuracy of HR-based intensity prescription can be compromised when estimations of HRmax are used or with the use of medications known to influence HR (i.e., β-blockers). Medication use can be identified from the initial health history and should be updated as needed when changes in medications occur.
summary: Heart rate can be used to prescribe exercise intensity, but estimations of HRmax and medications can affect accuracy.

content: When a measured HRmax, obtained during a graded exercise test, is unavailable, the Personal Trainer commonly uses an age-predicted estimate (220 − age). The ‘220 − age’ equation for estimating HRmax can underestimate or overestimate measured HRmax and has a variability of ± 10–12 bpm (i.e., 1 standard deviation). Population-specific equations for estimating HRmax are available and may be superior to the ‘220 − age’ equation in some individuals. Although directly measured HRmax is preferred to estimated methods, when this is not feasible, an estimation using one of these methods is acceptable. When estimating HRmax, choose an equation that most represents the client population.
summary: HRmax can be estimated using various equations, with the '220 - age' method being common but potentially inaccurate. Population-specific equations may be more accurate.

content: The Astrand equation for HRmax is HRmax = 216.6 − (0.84 × age) for men and women aged 4−34 years. The Tanaka et al. equation for HRmax is HRmax = 208 − (0.7 × age) for healthy men and women. The Gellish et al. equation for HRmax is HRmax = 207 − (0.7 × age) for men and women participants in an adult fitness program with a broad range of ages and fitness levels. The Gulati et al. equation for HRmax is HRmax = 206 − (0.88 × age) for asymptomatic middle-aged women referred for stress testing.
summary: Several equations exist for estimating HRmax, each tailored to specific populations.

content: ACSM recommends a percentage of HRmax between 64% and 76% for moderate-intensity exercise and 77% to 95% for vigorous intensity. The calculation for the percentage of target HR (lower end of range) for moderate intensity is (HRmax) × 0.64. The calculation for the percentage of target HR (lower end of range) for moderate intensity is (HRmax) × 0.76.
summary: ACSM recommends specific HRmax percentages for moderate and vigorous intensity exercise.

content: For a 20-year-old with an estimated HRmax of 200 bpm, the range for moderate-intensity exercise is 128–152 bpm. The Personal Trainer should consider the client’s goals, health history, and current fitness level when determining the exercise intensity range. For a moderately active 20-year-old client, the target HR range could be 128–190 bpm. For a very deconditioned or unfit client, a lower percentage (57%–63% HRmax) should be used for light intensity. It is important for the Personal Trainer to not overestimate the client’s ability and start at too high of an intensity. It is often more effective to start with a conservative intensity and increase it as needed.
summary: Exercise intensity ranges are individualized based on client factors and should be adjusted gradually.

content: HRR is a measure of relative intensity because it uses information relative to the client’s peak or HRmax and HRrest. A lower HRrest can be an indicator of greater CRF. HRR, often referred to as the Karvonen method or formula, is based on the difference between HRmax and HRrest.
summary: HRR, calculated as the difference between HRmax and HRrest, is a relative measure of exercise intensity.

content: It is often more effective to start with a conservative intensity and increase it as needed.  HRR is a measure of relative intensity because it uses information relative to the client’s peak or HRmax and HRrest. A lower HRrest can be an indicator of greater CRF. HRR, often referred to as the Karvonen method or formula, is based on the difference between HRmax and HRrest. For moderate- to vigorous-intensity exercise, the intensity range is 40%–89% HRR. The calculation for the percentage of target HRR (lower end of moderate range) is [(HRmax ‒ HRrest) × 0.40] + HRrest. The calculation for the percentage of target HRR (upper end of vigorous range) is [(HRmax ‒ HRrest) × 0.89] + HRrest. For a 20-year-old client with an HRrest of 75 bpm, the range for exercise intensity using HRR is 125–186 bpm.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of Heart Rate Reserve (HRR) as a measure of exercise intensity, including its calculation and application for moderate to vigorous exercise.

content: Rating of perceived exertion (RPE) is used to subjectively rate overall feelings of exertion during exercise and can be helpful in guiding exercise intensity. Commonly used RPE scales include the Borg 6–20 scale and the Borg Category Ratio 10 (CR10) scale. The threshold level for cardiorespiratory benefits appears to be an RPE of 12–13, which represents moderate intensity. When using the Borg 6–20 scale, it is important for the Personal Trainer to explain how to use the scale and to ‘anchor’ certain numbers on the scale so the client has a point of reference. When explaining how to use the Borg 6–20 scale, the Personal Trainer should remind the client that the number he or she is giving represents overall body fatigue and is not localized to a specific area of the body. When anchoring the scale numbers, a 9 represents ‘very light’ activity and can equate to a healthy individual walking slowly at his or her own pace. A 13 on the Borg 6–20 scale represents an intensity that is ‘somewhat hard’ and means that the exercise is hard, but he or she can still continue. When using RPE, the Personal Trainer should keep in mind the variability between individuals (e.g., the RPE value will not necessarily correspond directly with a particular percentage of HRmax or percentage of HRR) and then make adjustments as needed. RPE is often recommended for determining exercise intensity in older adults and is helpful for individuals having difficulty assessing their HR or who are taking medications, which influence HR.
summary: This chunk describes the use of Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) as a method for gauging exercise intensity, including specific scales, anchoring techniques, and considerations for different populations.

content: The talk test is a reliable method to estimate exercise intensity and is associated with the transition from aerobic to anaerobic energy systems via lactate threshold, ventilatory threshold, and the respiratory compensation point. How well a client is able to speak or carry on a conversation relates well to his or her relative intensity. The Personal Trainer should first estimate the client’s moderate- to vigorous-intensity HR range using the HRR method. Next, have the client perform a 5- to 10-minute warm-up and then gradually increase the intensity to a walking or jogging speed. When the client reaches a steady-state HR at the new intensity, have them recite a standard phrase aloud (e.g., the 31-word United States Pledge of Allegiance). After saying the phrase, have the client subjectively assess his or her level of speech difficulty based on Table 15.5.
summary: This chunk explains the talk test as a method for estimating exercise intensity, outlining the process and its connection to physiological thresholds.

content: Exercise duration and intensity are typically inversely related. As one increases, the other decreases. ACSM recommends 30 min ∙ d−1 (150 min ∙ wk−1) of moderate-intensity exercise, 20 min ∙ d−1 (75 min ∙ wk−1) of vigorous exercise, or a combination of both to improve CRF. This duration can be in one exercise session or could be accomplished intermittently through multiple exercise bouts. If fitness gains are a primary goal and the exercise is done intermittently, then each exercise bout should be ≥10 minutes for each bout. Current evidence suggests that accumulating physical activity in bouts that are <10 minutes in duration are also associated with favorable health benefits, including a reduction in all-cause mortality. Sedentary individuals may need to begin with very short bouts (i.e., 5–10 min ∙ d−1) of low-intensity exercise. Time and intensity should then be gradually increased while always being mindful of the client’s fatigue level. The rate of progression will vary depending on the health status and age of the individual. Longer durations of exercise (≥30–60 min ∙ d−1 or more; 250–300 min ∙ wk−1) are recommended for weight control especially for those who are otherwise sedentary. This time frame does not include warm-up and cool-down, both of which should be completed in addition to the time spent at the target exercise program.
summary: This chunk outlines exercise duration and intensity recommendations based on fitness goals and individual factors, including the benefits of shorter exercise bouts.

content: The type of exercise selected should be based on the client’s fitness, health, skill, and interests. During the initial consultation with a client, it is best to discuss what activities are most enjoyed as well as those that are accessible. Enjoyability and access may seem obvious but are important to consider when selecting an exercise mode for best possible adherence, particularly outside of the sessions with the Personal Trainer. Typically, cardiorespiratory exercises are those that involve the use of large muscle groups in a repetitive, rhythmic fashion. Some activities are weight bearing, meaning that body weight is moved during the exercise (e.g., walking, running). In other activities, body weight is not a factor because the body is supported (e.g., cycling, swimming) and are referred to as non–weight-bearing exercises.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of selecting exercises based on client preferences, abilities, and accessibility for long-term adherence.

content: During the initial consultation with a client, it is best to discuss what activities are most enjoyed as well as those that are accessible. Enjoyability and access may seem obvious but are important to consider when selecting an exercise mode for best possible adherence, particularly outside of the sessions with the Personal Trainer. As a result, Group D activities should be used with caution for clients with low fitness or who are at high risk or symptomatic for disease unless modifications to rules are implemented. Group D activities are generally recommended as ancillary activities that are added to a regular fitness program (10). The Personal Trainer and the client must maintain open lines of communication regarding the selection of exercise modes. Clients should move to Group B if they have the skills or desire to learn new skills to include Group C activities or enjoy the variety and challenge of Group D activities.
summary: Client consultation should prioritize enjoyable and accessible activities for adherence. Group D activities should be used cautiously with less fit clients, and open communication is key for exercise mode selection.
