content: The ability to recover from submaximal exercise is impaired in the aging population. Blood lactate levels remain higher in an aging population compared to a young population postexercise. Total body water declines with increasing age contributing to decreased blood volume and impaired thirst sensation. In males, total body water decreases about 0.3 kg per year after age 30 years, and in females, by 0.7 kg per year after age 70 years. These changes predispose the older person to reduced exercise capacity, dehydration, and impaired exercise tolerance in hot and humid weather.
summary: Aging affects exercise recovery, blood lactate clearance, fluid balance, and thermoregulation, potentially limiting exercise performance and tolerance.

content: Between age 19 and 79 years, there is a progressive decline in brain blood flow and a decrease in brain weight. This is likely due to loss of fluid and the slowing of nerve conduction velocity resulting in slower reaction times and slower voluntary movements. These decrements in nerve conduction velocity are thought to contribute to some of the losses in muscular strength and power that occur with advancing age.
summary: Age-related changes in the brain, including reduced blood flow and nerve conduction velocity, contribute to slower reaction times, movement, and muscle function.

content: The main culprit in decreased muscular strength with increasing age is a 30%–50% decrease in muscle mass between age 30 and 80 years due to a decrease in the number of muscle fibers and greater atrophy of type II (fast-twitch) muscle fibers compared with type I fibers. As muscular power is a function of both strength and speed of movement, these muscle fiber changes mean that power output declines at a faster rate than strength alone. Lower body strength declines more rapidly than upper body strength. Muscular endurance also declines with age, although it does so at a slower rate than strength.
summary: Age-related muscle loss, particularly of fast-twitch fibers, contributes to decreased strength, power, and endurance.

content: Body weight actually increases in the 30s, 40s, and 50s through a progressive accumulation of body fat, particularly in the abdominal region. After age 70 years, body weight starts to decline.
summary: Body composition changes with age, with increased fat mass in middle age and potential weight loss in later years.

content: The prevalence of hypertension in Americans older than 50 years of age accounts for the majority of cases of uncontrolled hypertension. Those with “normal” blood pressure at age 55 and 65 years have a 90% risk of developing hypertension over the remainder of their life.
summary: Hypertension becomes increasingly prevalent with age, affecting a majority of older adults and posing a significant health risk.

content: About 50% of the decrements noted earlier are due to actual aging, whereas the other 50% are due to sedentary living, poor nutrition, tobacco use, and alcohol consumption, which can be altered through exercise. Limiting time spent sitting and participation in physical activity reduces the risk of developing major chronic diseases common in older adults.
summary: Lifestyle factors significantly contribute to age-related decline, and exercise can mitigate these effects and reduce chronic disease risk.

content: Degeneration of the elastic components of connective tissue leads to a loss of mobility and stability in the joints. Body weight actually increases in the 30s, 40s, and 50s through a progressive accumulation of body fat, particularly in the abdominal region. After age 70 years, body weight starts to decline.
summary: This chunk describes age-related changes in body composition and joint health.

content: About 50% of the decrements noted earlier are due to actual aging, whereas the other 50% are due to sedentary living, poor nutrition, tobacco use, and alcohol consumption, which can be altered through exercise. Limiting time spent sitting and participation in physical activity reduces the risk of developing major chronic diseases common in older adults.
summary: This chunk highlights the impact of lifestyle factors on aging and the benefits of exercise.

content: Recent findings indicate multicomponent activity programs including aerobic, muscle-strengthening, and balance activities to be appropriate and encouraged for all older adults. Being regularly active throughout life not only significantly minimizes the normal age-related changes but can also increase aerobic capacity and muscular strength by 20%-30% in older adults.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of a comprehensive exercise program for older adults.

content: In older adults, aerobic capacity, muscular strength, body composition, and flexibility improvements are quite similar to those seen in young adults when compared based on relative change (relative to maximal capacity) and can occur at least into the 70s. Aerobic training may actually improve exercise efficiency to a greater extent in the elderly as compared with the young, although older adults are not able to increase their lower body strength to the same magnitude as young adults.
summary: This chunk compares the benefits of exercise for older and younger adults.

content: Exercise not only improves health-related quality of life and psychological well-being but also increases the length of life. Physical inactivity is among the strongest predictors of physical disability in older adults.
summary: This chunk highlights the overall health benefits of exercise for older adults.

content: A meta-analysis of 38 studies found that regular physical activity at a mild to moderate intensity was strongly associated with a reduction in all-cause mortality in active compared with sedentary individuals. In everyday life, functional tasks such as getting up out of a chair, climbing stairs, bringing in the groceries, and keeping the house clean require at least minimal levels of cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, endurance and power, flexibility, and balance.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the link between physical activity and mortality risk, and the importance of functional fitness in daily life.

content: Regular involvement in aerobic, anaerobic, resistance, flexibility, and functional training is a key element of successful aging. The likelihood is high that older adults will have clinically significant or underlying chronic disease.
summary: This chunk outlines the components of a comprehensive exercise program for successful aging, acknowledging the prevalence of chronic disease in older adults.

content: A sedentary, asymptomatic older person can initiate low- to moderate-intensity exercise without medical evaluation. For sedentary older adults with known cardiovascular, metabolic, or renal disease and/or signs or symptoms suggestive of these diseases, a medical clearance is recommended before starting an exercise program.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on when medical clearance is necessary for older adults starting an exercise program.

content: Exercise testing can determine functional capacity, help establish a safe exercise prescription, and can enable monitoring of progress in an exercise program. The main objective for working with older clients is to enhance and support “successful aging.”
summary: This chunk highlights the role of exercise testing and the overarching goal of exercise for older adults.

content: Personal Trainers should design programs for older adults with three primary goals in mind: 1. Prevent or delay the progression of chronic diseases (and/or possibly “reverse” symptoms as in normalizing blood glucose). 2. Maintain or enhance cardiorespiratory fitness levels (i.e., functional capacity). 3. Prevent functional limitations and disabilities.
summary: This chunk outlines the three primary goals of exercise programming for older adults.

content: Cardiorespiratory fitness is arguably the most important goal of an exercise program for older adults. Low cardiorespiratory fitness contributes to premature mortality in middle-aged and older adults. For every one metabolic equivalent (1-MET) improvement in cardiorespiratory fitness, one may expect a 25% reduction in overall mortality risk.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of cardiorespiratory fitness for older adults.

content: Enhanced cardiorespiratory fitness improves insulin sensitivity, blood lipid profile, body composition, blood pressure, and autonomic nervous system function, leading to a decreased overall risk for developing cardiovascular disease. The inevitable decline in physiological function, in this case cardiorespiratory fitness, has been delayed.
summary: This chunk details the benefits of improved cardiorespiratory fitness for older adults.

content: Being regularly active throughout life not only significantly minimizes the normal age-related changes but also restores functional capacity in previously sedentary adults.
summary: This chunk highlights the long-term benefits of regular physical activity.

content: An exercise program should be based on exercise testing results, consider the person’s preferences and capabilities, be individualized with attainable goals, and provide regular and meaningful feedback as support for long-term adherence.
summary: This chunk outlines the key principles for designing an effective exercise program for older adults.

content: When working with an older client, plan a warm-up (minimum of 5–10 min) with gentle dynamic stretching to gradually ease the person from rest to the chosen exercise intensity. A deliberate cool-down (minimum of 5–10 min) should gradually bring the person back to resting levels after exercise.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on warm-up and cool-down procedures for older adults.

content: Time spent in warm-up and cool-down count toward meeting the ACSM physical activity guidelines, as long as the activity is at least moderate intensity (i.e., brisk walking).
summary: This chunk clarifies the inclusion of warm-up and cool-down time in physical activity guidelines.

content: In general, physical activity programs for older adults should be designed to meet the current recommendations of at least 150–300 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity a week, spread out across at least 5 days, with a target of 30 min ∙ d−1 (even greater benefits are shown for up to 60 min ∙ d−1). If completing vigorous exercise, then the recommendation is a target of 20–30 min ∙ d−1 on 3 or more days per week accumulating 75–100 minutes of vigorous-intensity activity per week.
summary: This chunk outlines the recommended guidelines for physical activity for older adults.

content: The exercise does not need to be continuous but can be accumulated in smaller bouts across the day. An equivalent combination of moderate and vigorous activity can also be prescribed.
summary: This chunk provides flexibility in meeting physical activity guidelines.

content: Research has found that aerobic physical activity is most beneficial in reducing the risk of injury and preventing excess fatigue when spread throughout the week on at least 3 days (or more for greater benefit). When not feasible for older adults to fulfill these guidelines due to debilitating chronic conditions, Personal Trainers should encourage these individuals to be as physically active as their condition permits.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of spreading out exercise throughout the week and encourages activity within individual limitations.

content: Exercise intensity for older adults is defined relative to individual fitness using a perceived physical exertion scale of 0–10 for which 0 is equivalent to sitting and 10 is maximal effort. Moderate-intensity activity is considered as a 5 or 6 and vigorous-intensity activity as a 7 or 8. However, relative intensity individualized to the person should be used to prescribe aerobic training.
summary: This chunk defines exercise intensity for older adults and emphasizes the importance of individualization.

content: Walking is the most common activity for older adults, although any activity that does not present excessive orthopedic stress can be included.
summary: This chunk provides examples of suitable activities for older adults.

content: When not feasible for older adults to fulfill these guidelines due to debilitating chronic conditions, Personal Trainers should encourage these individuals to be as physically active as their condition permits.  Exercise intensity for older adults is defined relative to individual fitness using a perceived physical exertion scale of 0–10 for which 0 is equivalent to sitting and 10 is maximal effort. Moderate-intensity activity is considered as a 5 or 6 and vigorous-intensity activity as a 7 or 8. However, relative intensity individualized to the person should be used to prescribe aerobic training. Walking is the most common activity for older adults, although any activity that does not present excessive orthopedic stress can be included.
summary: Exercise prescription for older adults should be individualized and consider their fitness level and limitations.

content: Recent findings suggest multicomponent physical activities to be the most beneficial for specific physical function outcomes such as strength, gait speed, balance, and activities of daily living. Multicomponent activities include more than one type of physical activity including aerobic, muscle strengthening, and balance training. Walking is an excellent mode of exercise for many older adults. Modality for exercise should not impose excessive orthopedic stress. Aquatic, stationary cycle, and recumbent stepper exercise may be preferable for clients with a diminished ability to tolerate weight-bearing exercise.
summary: Multicomponent activities, including walking and low-impact options, are recommended for older adults to improve various physical functions.

content: Modality should be accessible, convenient, and enjoyable to promote adherence to exercise. A group setting may provide social reinforcement to adherence to exercise. To minimize complications and promote long-term compliance, intensity for inactive older adults should start low and progress according to client preference and tolerance. Many adults have clinically diagnosed conditions or likely have underlying chronic diseases; thus, a conservative approach to increasing exercise intensity may be required. Exercise need not be vigorous and continuous to be beneficial.
summary: Exercise modality and intensity should be tailored to individual needs and preferences to ensure adherence and safety.

content: To prevent injury, ensure safety, and promote adherence, older adults should increase exercise duration prior to intensity. Duration need not be continuous to produce benefits. Clients who have difficulty sustaining exercise for 30 minutes or who prefer shorter bouts of exercise can be advised to exercise for 10-minute bouts throughout the day. A daily accumulation of 30 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity can provide health benefits. Even greater benefits are possible with up to 60 minutes ∙ d−1 of moderate-intensity physical activity.
summary: Exercise duration can be accumulated in shorter bouts throughout the day to achieve recommended guidelines.

content: Moderate-intensity exercise should be performed on 5 or more days per week, vigorous-intensity activity on 3 or more days per week, or a combination of moderate and vigorous on 3–5 d ∙ wk−1. Aging is associated with a reduction in muscle mass, which in turn contributes to decreased muscle strength and a decline in functional capacity. The process of reduced muscle mass can ultimately result in reduced balance ability, mobility problems, and lack of independence for the older adult. Decreased muscle mass plays a role in the development of glucose intolerance and Type 2 diabetes.
summary: Older adults should engage in regular moderate to vigorous physical activity, and resistance training is crucial to combat age-related muscle loss.

content: The Personal Trainer should recognize the importance of implementing a resistance training program for older adult clients to attenuate the loss of muscle mass and protein. Muscle fiber size and performance, particularly the rate of force development, are consistently higher in older adults who are chronically exposed to strength training. Some studies have suggested that resistance training can improve cognition, mood, self-confidence, and self-esteem.
summary: Resistance training is essential for older adults to maintain muscle mass, strength, and overall well-being.

content: The general resistance training principles detailed earlier can be applied when designing a resistance training program for older individuals. In certain elderly clients, even explosive-type, heavy resistance exercise has been shown to be safe and effective. The Personal Trainer should provide sufficient instruction on proper lifting technique and good posture. Initially, beginners may benefit from using weight training or resistance machines.
summary: Resistance training programs for older adults should be tailored to their abilities and include proper instruction on technique.

content: Weight training machines leave less room for error in body position, and they safely enable larger absolute loads to be lifted. If weight-training machines are used with small-framed or frail clients, care must be taken that they fit the individual properly. Other modalities such as free weights (e.g., dumbbells), elastic bands, and body weight activities can offer different benefits for those older participants who are experienced in their use.
summary: Various resistance training modalities can be used for older adults, with considerations for individual needs and experience levels.

content: Some major advantages for these types of exercise modalities are that they are inexpensive, can be done at home, and take up minimal space. They can promote kinesthetic awareness and help improve balance.
summary: Home-based exercises using free weights, bands, or bodyweight can be effective and accessible for older adults.

content: Measured peak heart rate is preferable to age-predicted peak heart rate because of the variability in peak heart rate in clients >65 years and their greater risk for underlying coronary artery disease (CAD). Activities performed at a given MET level represent greater relative intensities in older adults than in younger clients because of the decrease in peak METs with age. Older adults are likely to be taking medications that can influence heart rate.
summary: Heart rate monitoring and exercise intensity prescription should consider individual factors and potential medication effects in older adults.

content: Intensity on a level of physical exertion should be 5–6 for moderate intensity and 7–8 for vigorous, on a scale from 0 to 10.
summary: Exercise intensity for older adults should be measured using a perceived exertion scale.

content: Weight training machines leave less room for error in body position, and they safely enable larger absolute loads to be lifted. If weight-training machines are used with small-framed or frail clients, care must be taken that they fit the individual properly.
summary: Weight training machines offer safety and load benefits, but proper fit is crucial for smaller clients.

content: Other modalities such as free weights (e.g., dumbbells), elastic bands, and body weight activities can offer different benefits for those older participants who are experienced in their use. Some major advantages for these types of exercise modalities are that they are inexpensive, can be done at home, and take up minimal space. They can promote kinesthetic awareness and help improve balance.
summary: Free weights, bands, and bodyweight exercises offer accessible and versatile benefits for experienced older adults.

content: Older individuals are at a greater risk of falls. Power training, a nonconventional type of resistance training, may be beneficial in older adults. Power training has been shown to be beneficial in reducing the risk of falls. To increase skeletal muscle power in older individuals, the Personal Trainer should have his or her client do high-velocity exercises including one to three sets of single and multijoint exercises at 30%-60% of 1-RM for 6-10 repetitions. Older participants should move through the full range of motion using proper form and avoid breath holding during the lifts.
summary: Power training can reduce fall risk in older adults by improving muscle power and encouraging proper movement.

content: Intensity can be prescribed between moderate (5-6 on the 10-point exertion scale) and vigorous (23). If the client’s 1-RM is known, the target is 60%-70% 1-RM, although lower intensities (e.g., 40%-50% 1-RM) are appropriate for beginners. Generally, one set of 10-15 repetitions is recommended when working with older adults. One set of 8 to 10 exercises including the major muscle groups should be part of the resistance training program, although two to three sets may be more beneficial.
summary: Resistance training intensity and volume should be tailored to the individual, considering factors like 1-RM and experience level.

content: As with any resistance training prescription, load should be increased when the number of repetitions that can be completed with proper form exceeds the initially prescribed number.
summary: Progressive overload is essential for continued strength gains in resistance training.

content: Flexibility is an essential component of fitness and decreases with age and physical inactivity. As was discussed in Chapter 16 with increasing age, the ability to move through a full range of motion becomes compromised. Aging causes connective tissue to become stiffer, which makes the joint more resistant to bending. The joint cartilage, ligaments, tendons, synovial fluid, and muscles begin to deteriorate, which may decrease joint range of motion. The loss of joint flexibility with aging has been reported to range from negligible to 57% with the majority of this loss occurring by age 65 years.
summary: Flexibility naturally declines with age due to changes in connective tissue and joint structures.

content: Poor flexibility, coupled with decreased musculoskeletal strength, has been associated with a diminished ability to perform activities of daily living. Static stretching should only be done when muscles and joints are warm. Static stretching done immediately prior to exercise could have potentially detrimental effects in some instances (i.e., decreased muscle strength and endurance, impaired balance, and diminished reaction time.)
summary: Decreased flexibility can limit daily activities, and stretching should be done safely and strategically.

content: To improve flexibility, older adults should gently increase the length of muscle beyond that used in everyday activities at least 2 d ∙ wk−1. This can be done dynamically, statically, or both. Static held positions to stretch the muscles are to be held for 10-30 seconds and stretching should be employed for all the major muscle groups in the body.
summary: Older adults should incorporate regular stretching into their routine to maintain and improve flexibility.

content: Fall incidence rates currently pose a serious health problem for older adults with 1 in every 4 individuals aged 65 years and older experiencing one fall a year in the United States. Decreased balance with aging can be attributable to a series of declines in multiple physiological and psychological systems. Flexibility (ROM), muscular strength, reduced central processing of sensory information, slowed motor responses, and declines in executive function are associated with aging (72). With aging, myelin sheaths sometimes exhibit degenerative change, and there is a loss of nerve fibers from the white matter in the brain (73). These changes contribute to a decline in sensory capability and cognitive function.
summary: Falls are a significant risk for older adults due to age-related declines in balance, sensory perception, and cognitive function.

content: Cognitive executive function is a key factor in maintaining independence in older years (74). Sensory capability is also affected by increasing age. Sight, hearing, taste, balance, vestibular function, and proprioception decline in old age, leading to a greater risk of falling (41).
summary: Cognitive function and sensory abilities decline with age, contributing to fall risk.

content: Physical activity programs reduce the risk of fall-related injuries in older adults (1). Community-structured programs may be beneficial in long-term exercise participation to promote overall well-being, physical function, and reduce fall risk.
summary: Regular physical activity can help mitigate fall risk and improve overall health in older adults.

content: It is recommended to incorporate multicomponent physical activity including gait, coordination, and physical function, as well as recreational activities of yoga, tai chi, gardening, and sports (23). Balance ability and postural control are critical factors for performing ADLs and participating in leisure-time activities (1). Balance and postural stability can be enhanced by combining flexibility with resistance exercise (75,76). Personal Trainers should therefore include balance exercises in older adults’ exercise programs.
summary: Exercise programs for older adults should include a variety of activities that target balance, coordination, and flexibility.

content: Community-structured programs may be beneficial in long-term exercise participation to promote overall well-being, physical function, and reduce fall risk. It is recommended to incorporate multicomponent physical activity including gait, coordination, and physical function, as well as recreational activities of yoga, tai chi, gardening, and sports (23). Balance ability and postural control are critical factors for performing ADLs and participating in leisure-time activities (1).
summary: Community programs and multicomponent activities can promote long-term exercise participation and well-being, emphasizing balance and postural control for daily activities and leisure.

content: Balance and postural stability can be enhanced by combining flexibility with resistance exercise (75,76). Personal Trainers should therefore include balance exercises in older adults’ exercise programs. Both static and dynamic balance activities should be employed at least twice a week. Progression of balance exercises should occur in a safe environment where a fall would not injure the participant (having spotters, having nearby secure objects to hang on to, and using mats). Progression can take place by steadily reducing the dynamic or static base of support (52).
summary: Balance training for older adults should include both static and dynamic exercises, progressing gradually in a safe environment to improve stability.

content: Although research has yet to identify the optimal frequency, duration, and type of balance exercises, balance training may be performed 2–3 d ∙ wk−1 (11,23). Balance training can be integrated into various phases of the exercise session, including warm-up, main component, or cool-down. It is critical that Personal Trainers include balance exercises in older adults’ exercise programs.
summary: Balance training can be incorporated 2-3 times per week and integrated into various exercise phases, emphasizing its importance for older adults.

content: Personal Trainers should be aware of special considerations regarding exercise program design for special populations to create effective (and safe) exercise regimens for their clientele. By being aware of and using, the evidence-based guidelines noted in this chapter for pregnancy, children, and older adults, the Personal Trainer will minimize risk of injury during exercise and develop a more individualized exercise plan for his or her specific clientele population.
summary: Personal trainers should consider special populations and evidence-based guidelines when designing exercise programs to ensure safety and effectiveness.

content: Forsum E and Löf M wrote a paper on energy metabolism during human pregnancy in 2007. Artal R, O’Toole M, and White S wrote guidelines for exercise during pregnancy and the postpartum period for the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists in 2003. Gregg VH and Ferguson JE 2nd wrote a paper on exercise in pregnancy in 2017. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists released Committee Opinion No. 650 summary on physical activity and exercise during pregnancy and the postpartum period in 2015.
summary: Various publications and guidelines address exercise recommendations during pregnancy and the postpartum period, emphasizing the importance of safe and effective exercise practices.

content: The American College of Sports Medicine published ACSM’s Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription in its 11th edition in 2022. Savvaki D, Taousani E, Goulis DG, et al. wrote a review of international recommendations for exercise during normal pregnancy and gestational diabetes in 2018. Wolfe LA and Mottola M created PARmed-X for Pregnancy: Physical Activity Readiness Medical Examination in 2015. The U.S. Department of Health and Human Services published 2008 Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans in 2008.
summary: Numerous publications and guidelines provide recommendations for exercise testing, prescription, and physical activity, including specific considerations for pregnancy and overall health.

content: Rodriguez-Ayllon M, Cadenas-Sánchez C, Estévez-López F, et al. conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis on the role of physical activity and sedentary behavior in the mental health of preschoolers, children and adolescents in 2019. The American College of Sports Medicine published ACSM’s Exercise Management for Persons with Chronic Diseases and Disabilities in its 4th edition in 2016.
summary: Research and guidelines explore the impact of physical activity and sedentary behavior on mental health across various age groups, including recommendations for exercise management in individuals with chronic conditions.

content: Lloyd RS, Faigenbaum AD, Stone MH, et al. published a position statement on youth resistance training in 2014. Faigenbaum AD, Kraemer WJ, Blimkie CJ, et al. published an updated position statement paper on youth resistance training from the National Strength and Conditioning Association in 2009. Faigenbaum AD, Lloyd RS, and Myer GD wrote on youth resistance training: past practices, new perspectives, and future directions in 2013.
summary: Position statements and publications address the evolving landscape of youth resistance training, emphasizing best practices and future directions.

content: Kwak S, Kim H, Chey J, and Youm Y found that subjective age is associated with estimated brain age in 2018. Murphy SL, Xu J, Kochanek KD, and Arias E published Mortality in the United States, 2017 in 2018. Taylor D published Americans with Disabilities: 2014 in 2018.
summary: Research explores the relationship between subjective age, brain age, mortality rates, and disability prevalence, providing insights into aging trends and demographics.

content: Paneni F, Diaz Cañestro C, Libby P, Lüscher TF, and Camici GG wrote on the aging cardiovascular system in 2017. Vasan RS, Beiser A, Seshadri S, et al. determined the residual lifetime risk for developing hypertension in middle-aged women and men in the Framingham Heart Study in 2002. Ferrari AU, Radaelli A, and Centola M wrote an invited review on aging and the cardiovascular system in 2003. Gellish RL, Goslin BR, Olson RE, McDonald A, Russi GD, and Moudgil VK modeled the relationship between age and maximal heart rate in 2007.
summary: Studies investigate the aging cardiovascular system, including risk factors, physiological changes, and the relationship between age and heart rate.

content: Tanaka H, Monahan KD, and Seals DR revisited age-predicted maximal heart rate in 2001. Tanaka H and Seals DR wrote on endurance exercise performance in Masters athletes in 2008. Daley MJ and Spinks WL wrote on exercise, mobility and aging in 2000. Fleg JL, Morrell CH, Bos AG, et al. found accelerated longitudinal decline of aerobic capacity in healthy older adults in 2005. Deschenes MR, Carter JA, Matney EN, Potter MB, and Wilson MH found that aged men experience disturbances in recovery following submaximal exercise in 2006.
summary: Research examines age-related changes in maximal heart rate, endurance performance, mobility, and recovery from exercise in older adults.

content: The American College of Sports Medicine published a position stand on Exercise and physical activity for older adults in 2009. Melamed E, Lavy S, Bentin S, Cooper G, and Rinot Y found a reduction in regional cerebral blood flow during normal aging in man in 1980.
summary: Guidelines and research highlight the importance of exercise for older adults, including the impact of aging on cerebral blood flow.

content: Daley MJ and Spinks WL wrote on exercise, mobility and aging in 2000. The American College of Sports Medicine published a position stand on Exercise and physical activity for older adults in 2009. The American College of Sports Medicine published *ACSM’s Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription* 10th edition in 2018.
summary: This chunk highlights key publications and guidelines related to exercise and aging.

content: Fleg JL, Morrell CH, Bos AG, et al. found accelerated longitudinal decline of aerobic capacity in healthy older adults in 2005. Wilson TM and Tanaka H published a meta-analysis on the age-associated decline in maximal aerobic capacity in men in relation to training status in the *Am J Physiol Heart Circ Physiol* in 2000. Jackson AS, Sui X, Hébert JR, Church TS, and Blair SN published a study on the role of lifestyle and aging on the longitudinal change in cardiorespiratory fitness in the *Arch Intern Med* in 2009. Hollmann W, Strüder HK, Tagarakis CVM, and King G published a study on physical activity and the elderly in the *Eur J Cardiovasc Prev Rehabil* in 2007. Sui X, LaMonte MJ, Laditka JN, et al. published a study on cardiorespiratory fitness and adiposity as mortality predictors in older adults in the *JAMA* in 2007. Lee D-C, Artero EG, Sui X, and Blair SN published a study on mortality trends in the general population: the importance of cardiorespiratory fitness in the *J Psychopharmacol* in 2010. Imboden MT, Harber MP, Whaley MH, et al. published a study on the influence of change in cardiorespiratory fitness with short-term exercise training on mortality risk from the Ball State Adult Fitness Longitudinal Lifestyle Study in the *Mayo Clin Proc* in 2019.
summary: This chunk focuses on research findings and studies concerning the decline of aerobic capacity and mortality risk in older adults.

content: Deschenes MR, Carter JA, Matney EN, Potter MB, and Wilson MH found that aged men experience disturbances in recovery following submaximal exercise in 2006.
summary: This chunk highlights a specific study on recovery disturbances in aged men after submaximal exercise.

content: Melamed E, Lavy S, Bentin S, Cooper G, and Rinot Y found a reduction in regional cerebral blood flow during normal aging in man in 1980. Creasey H and Rapoport SI wrote on the aging human brain in 1985. Peters A published a review on the effects of normal aging on myelin and nerve fibers in *J Neurocytol* in 2002.
summary: This chunk explores the impact of aging on the human brain, including cerebral blood flow and nerve fiber health.

content: Palve SS and Palve SB wrote on the impact of aging on nerve conduction velocities and late responses in healthy individuals in 2018. Metter EJ, Conwit R, Metter B, Pacheco T, and Tobin J found the relationship of peripheral motor nerve conduction velocity to age-associated loss of grip strength in 1998. Strotmeyer ES, de Rekeneire N, Schwartz AV, et al. wrote on sensory and motor peripheral nerve function and lower-extremity quadriceps strength in 2009.
summary: This chunk focuses on the effects of aging on nerve conduction velocities and their relationship to muscle strength.

content: Koopman R and van Loon LJ wrote on aging, exercise, and muscle protein metabolism in 2009. Breen L and Phillips SM published a study on skeletal muscle protein metabolism in the elderly: interventions to counteract the ‘anabolic resistance’ of ageing in the *Nutr Metab (Lond)* in 2011. Aagaard P, Magnusson PS, Larsson B, Kjaer M, and Krustrup P published a study on mechanical muscle function, morphology, and fiber type in lifelong trained elderly in the *Med Sci Sports Exerc* in 2007.
summary: This chunk explores the interplay of aging, exercise, and muscle protein metabolism.

content: Arampatzis A, Degens H, Baltzopoulos V, and Rittweger J wrote on why older sprinters reach the finish line later in 2011.  Dishman RK, Berthoud HR, Booth FW, et al. published a study on the neurobiology of exercise in *Obesity (Silver Spring)* in 2006. Cadore EL, Pinto RS, Reischak-Oliveira Á, and Izquierdo M published a study on explosive type of contractions during resistance training in the elderly in the *Exp Gerontol* in 2018. Mayer F, Scharhag-Rosenberger F, Carlsohn A, Cassel M, Müller S, and Scharhag J published a study on the intensity and effects of strength training in the elderly in the *Dtsch Arztebl Int* in 2011.
summary: This chunk examines the performance of older sprinters and the effects of exercise on the elderly, including neurobiology and strength training.

content: Heckman GA and McKelvie RS wrote on cardiovascular aging and exercise in healthy older adults in 2008.
summary: This chunk focuses on the relationship between cardiovascular aging and exercise in healthy older adults.

content: Pahor M, Guralnik JM, Ambrosius WT, et al. published a study on the effect of structured physical activity on the prevention of major mobility disability in older adults in the *JAMA* in 2014. Löllgen H, Böckenhoff A, and Knapp G published a study on physical activity and all-cause mortality in the *Int J Sports Med* in 2009. Bird M, Hill KD, Ball M, Hetherington S, and Williams AD published a study on the long-term benefits of a multi-component exercise intervention to balance and mobility in healthy older adults in the *Arch Gerontol Geriatr* in 2011.
summary: This chunk explores the benefits of physical activity for older adults, including preventing mobility disability and improving balance and mobility.

content: Falls are the leading cause of injury and death in older Americans according to the Center for Disease Control and Prevention. The American Geriatrics Society, British Geriatrics Society, and American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons Panel on Falls Prevention published a guideline for the prevention of falls in older persons in the *J Am Geriatr Soc* in 2001.
summary: This chunk highlights the prevalence of falls in older adults and the importance of fall prevention guidelines.

content: Only 23.3% of Americans self-report meeting both the aerobic and muscle-strengthening physical activity guidelines (1). This value drops considerably to 9.6% when physical activity is characterized using physical activity monitoring devices (2). Physical inactivity is associated with numerous unhealthy conditions, including obesity, hypertension, gestational and Type 2 diabetes, and atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (CVD) and contributes annually to over 5.3 million global deaths (3). 121.5 million American adults have one or more types of CVD (4).
summary: This chunk emphasizes the low rates of physical activity adherence and the significant health risks associated with physical inactivity.

content: Chen FT, Etnier J, Wu C-H, Cho Y-M, Hung T-M, and Chang Y-K published a study on the dose-response relationship between exercise duration and executive function in older adults in 2018. Costa PB, Graves BS, Whitehurst M, and Jacobs PL published a study on the acute effects of different durations of static stretching on dynamic balance performance in the *J Strength Cond Res* in 2009. Weldon SM and Hill RH published a systematic review of the literature on the efficacy of stretching for prevention of exercise-related injury in *Man Ther* in 2003. Shrier I published a systematic and critical review of the literature on whether stretching improves performance in *Clin J Sport Med* in 2004.
summary: This chunk examines the effects of exercise duration, stretching, and its impact on balance, executive function, and injury prevention.

content: Physical inactivity is associated with numerous unhealthy conditions, including obesity, hypertension, gestational and Type 2 diabetes, and atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (CVD) and contributes annually to over 5.3 million global deaths (3). 121.5 million American adults have one or more types of CVD (4). Although the prevalence of CVD-related deaths has declined since the 1980s, it remains the leading cause of death in the United States (4). More than 360,000 Americans died of heart disease in 2016 (4). Patterns of nutrient intake and physical inactivity underlie the global epidemic of chronic diseases, including obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and Type 2 diabetes, which all serve as risk factors that contribute to the process of coronary artery disease (CAD).
summary: Physical inactivity and unhealthy lifestyle choices contribute to the global burden of chronic diseases, particularly cardiovascular disease (CVD).

content: Individuals who are able to reach the age of 50 years with no CVD risk factors have markedly higher survival rates than those with any combination of risk factors (5). Even those individuals who have only one risk factor at middle age are at a much higher risk for CVD and CAD than middle-aged people with no risk factors (5). Exercise programs that can effectively slow and even reverse the process of atherosclerosis can be designed even for individuals with known CAD.
summary: Individuals with CVD risk factors face higher mortality risks, but exercise can mitigate these risks and even reverse atherosclerosis.

content: Positive risk factor modification is the primary goal of an aerobic exercise program for clients with CVD. There is a dose-response relation between exercise and multiple health outcomes, including risk of CAD and all-cause mortality, obesity, dyslipidemia, Type 2 diabetes, and, perhaps most importantly, cardiorespiratory fitness (6,7). Maximal cardiorespiratory fitness, or the body’s maximal ability to consume oxygen to produce energy, is an important marker for health outcomes and risk stratification (6).
summary: Aerobic exercise for clients with CVD aims to modify risk factors and improve cardiorespiratory fitness, a key indicator of health.

content: Studies have consistently demonstrated an inverse relation between maximal volume of oxygen consumed per unit time (V̇ O2max) and CVD and total mortality in men and women across the lifespan (8,9). Risk for CVD is highest for individuals with low levels of cardiorespiratory fitness even when compared with those with traditional risk factors such as hypertension, dyslipidemia, or obesity (10). Each one metabolic equivalent increase (3.5 mL ∙ kg−1 ∙ min−1) in cardiorespiratory fitness can reduce the risk of CVD and all-cause mortality by 8%–25% (10,11).
summary: Higher cardiorespiratory fitness is associated with lower CVD risk, even surpassing the impact of traditional risk factors.

content: The College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) and U.S. Department of Health and Human Services have noted that the health benefits of a program are closely associated with total weekly energy expenditure (12,13). Studies have shown that exercise programs with energy expenditure of 14–23 kcal ∙ kg−1 ∙ wk−1 lead to significant improvements in cardiorespiratory fitness and other important risk factors for CAD, including dyslipidemia, body composition, and insulin sensitivity (14–19).
summary: Exercise programs with sufficient energy expenditure yield significant health benefits, including improved cardiorespiratory fitness and reduced CAD risk factors.

content: In some patients with CVD, high-intensity interval training (HIIT) may have potential for improving V̇ O2 peak as compared with standard continuous moderate-intensity exercises (20). Although HIIT is safe within this population when patients are on an optimized medication regimen, an in-depth clinical understanding of the patient’s condition and the effects of acute and chronic exercise are needed to safely guide the patient through a training session and promote long-term compliance.
summary: HIIT may offer benefits for improving cardiorespiratory fitness in CVD patients, but careful consideration and clinical expertise are crucial for safe implementation.

content: When working with those with heart disease, the Personal Trainer should design an individualized aerobic exercise prescription conforming to the FITT-VP principles and parameters (frequency, intensity, time, type, volume, and progression) to fulfill total weekly volume of exercise that has been shown to elicit positive adaptations to CAD risk factors. Achievement of this objective can positively modify the process of atherosclerosis and subsequently reduce the likelihood of future cardiac events and increase survival (21,22).
summary: Personal trainers should create individualized exercise prescriptions for CVD patients, focusing on achieving sufficient weekly volume to modify risk factors and improve outcomes.

content: Exercise training is relatively safe for the majority of clients with CVD provided that appropriate assessment and screening is performed before beginning the program. The likelihood of an adverse event can be markedly reduced with baseline assessments, preparticipation screening, patient education, and client adherence to established exercise recommendations (12,23). All clients with CVD should have their cardiovascular risk assessed by their physician and gain physician clearance prior to participating in a formal exercise program.
summary: Exercise is generally safe for CVD patients with proper screening, assessment, and medical clearance.

content: Many patients with CVD have a qualifying diagnosis (i.e., myocardial infarction within the last year, percutaneous coronary interventions, coronary artery bypass graft surgery, heart valve repair or replacement, stable angina, heart failure with a reduced ejection fraction, or heart transplantation) to enroll in an outpatient cardiac rehabilitation program, which has received a class I (i.e., highest recommendation provided by major health organizations based on evidence of efficacy) indication by the American Heart Association. Due to relatively low cardiac rehabilitation referral rates, the Personal Trainer is encouraged to promote these programs to clients that may not have been informed of their existence.
summary: Cardiac rehabilitation programs are highly recommended for CVD patients, and personal trainers should encourage their clients to participate.

content: A major recommendation for all cardiac rehabilitation participants is to perform “home” exercise on noncardiac rehabilitation session days. This can serve as a good opportunity to continue care with a Personal Trainer or wait until the client completes the cardiac rehabilitation program. In the case that a client has or is actively participating in a cardiac rehabilitation program, Personal Trainers should request information regarding the client’s participation, including number of sessions of cardiac rehabilitation completed, types, durations, and intensities of exercise performed as well as any history of adverse events in response to exercise or any special considerations/precautions that should be taken during exercise.
summary: Cardiac rehabilitation participants should continue exercising at home, and personal trainers should collaborate with their clients' rehabilitation programs.

content: Most of the cardiorespiratory assessments described in Chapter 12 can be provided to clients with CVD after proper screening and clinical evaluations including a recent clinical exercise test. The overarching goal of an aerobic exercise intervention is to increase the client’s cardiorespiratory fitness. The exercise stimulus must be at an intensity, duration, and frequency that presents a challenge to the cardiovascular, skeletal muscle, neural, and metabolic systems to initiate the adaptive process in the respective systems.
summary: Cardiorespiratory assessments and exercise interventions should be tailored to CVD patients, aiming to improve fitness and challenge physiological systems.

content: Failure to meet minimal threshold values may result in lack of a training effect, whereas increasing intensity and/or volume too quickly may increase the risk of injuries and other adverse events. Clients starting at a very low fitness level and/or are frail may need to perform intermittent exercise and gradually increase bout duration, decrease rest time, and progress to completing the desired duration of continuous exercise at moderate intensities, followed by a progressive increase in intensity. The frequency of exercise should start at 2–3 d ∙ wk−1, with the ultimate goal of performing 4–5 d ∙ wk−1 of aerobic exercise.
summary: Exercise prescription for CVD patients should be individualized, considering their fitness level and gradually progressing intensity and frequency.

content: The general principles of determining exercise intensity in patients with CVD closely follow prescription recommendations for apparently healthy adults, with the main caveat being that intensity is relative to the individual’s peak cardiorespiratory fitness. When exercise testing data is available, moderate (40%–59% oxygen uptake reserve [V̇ O2R] or heart rate reserve [HRR]) and vigorous (60%–80% V̇ O2R or HRR) intensity are recommended. Details regarding the conditions in which the exercise test was performed must be considered to appropriately create the respective intensity parameters.
summary: Exercise intensity for CVD patients should be individualized based on their peak cardiorespiratory fitness, with moderate and vigorous intensity levels recommended.

content: 1. Only 23.3% of Americans self-report meeting both the aerobic and muscle-strengthening physical activity guidelines (1). 2. This value drops considerably to 9.6% when physical activity is characterized using physical activity monitoring devices (2).
summary: A significant portion of Americans fail to meet physical activity guidelines, highlighting the need for improved adherence.

content: Bird M, Hill KD, Ball M, Hetherington S, and Williams AD published a study on the long-term benefits of a multi-component exercise intervention to balance and mobility in healthy older adults in the *Arch Gerontol Geriatr* in 2011.
summary: A study investigated the long-term benefits of exercise on balance and mobility in older adults.

content: Clients starting at a very low fitness level and/or are frail may need to perform intermittent exercise and gradually increase bout duration, decrease rest time, and progress to completing the desired duration of continuous exercise at moderate intensities, followed by a progressive increase in intensity.
summary: Exercise prescription for clients with low fitness levels involves gradual progression from intermittent to continuous exercise, increasing duration, decreasing rest, and progressively increasing intensity.

content: The frequency of exercise should start at 2–3 d ∙ wk−1, with the ultimate goal of performing 4–5 d ∙ wk−1 of aerobic exercise.
summary: Exercise frequency should begin at 2-3 days per week, aiming for 4-5 days per week ultimately.

content: The general principles of determining exercise intensity in patients with CVD closely follow prescription recommendations for apparently healthy adults, with the main caveat being that intensity is relative to the individual’s peak cardiorespiratory fitness.
summary: Exercise intensity prescription for patients with CVD generally follows recommendations for healthy adults, but should be relative to individual fitness levels.

content: When exercise testing data is available, moderate (40%–59% oxygen uptake reserve [V̇ O2R] or heart rate reserve [HRR]) and vigorous (60%–80% V̇ O2R or HRR) intensity are recommended.
summary: Exercise intensity recommendations for patients with CVD, when exercise testing data is available, include moderate (40-59% V̇ O2R or HRR) and vigorous (60-80% V̇ O2R or HRR) levels.

content: Details regarding the conditions in which the exercise test was performed must be considered to appropriately create the respective intensity parameters.
summary: Exercise intensity parameters should be tailored based on the conditions during the exercise test.

content: In many clinical settings, maximal exercise testing is not employed to generate an exercise prescription but rather used as a diagnostic tool to identify underlying causes of CVD-related symptoms.
summary: Exercise testing for CVD patients is often used for diagnosis rather than prescribing exercise.

content: This scenario commonly requires the patient to withhold any heart rate–limiting medications (i.e., β-blockers) to sufficiently ‘stress’ the heart.
summary: Exercise testing may require withholding heart rate-limiting medications to adequately stress the heart.

content: If heart rate–limiting medications are continued or added after the initial exercise test, heart rate data acquired during the test should not be used to develop a heart rate–based exercise prescription.
summary: Heart rate data from exercise tests should not be used for prescribing exercise if heart rate-limiting medications are used.

content: When an exercise test is not available or the condition in which the test was performed does not reflect the current state of the patient’s medication regimen, it is appropriate and recommended to use subjective ratings of effort to prescribe light (rating of perceived exertion [RPE] <12 reflecting <40% HRR), moderate/“somewhat hard” (RPE 12–13 reflecting 40%–59% HRR) or vigorous/“hard” (RPE 14–16 reflecting 60%–80% HRR) (12).
summary: When exercise testing is unavailable or conditions differ from the current medication regimen, subjective ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) should be used to prescribe exercise intensity.

content: In cases when patients may have misinterpreted the RPE scale, the talk test (see Chapter 15) is recommended to verify effort.
summary: The talk test can be used to verify exercise effort when RPE scales are misinterpreted.

content: It is also important to note the limitations of using age-predicted maximal heart rate formulas to create a heart rate–based exercise prescription.
summary: Age-predicted maximal heart rate formulas have limitations for exercise prescription.

content: Even if a formula derived from patient-specific populations (i.e., β-blocked vs. non–β-blocked or men vs. women) is used, a significant level of variation (standard deviations of 10–15 bpm) (24) across age groups greatly reduces the applicability of these formulas in exercise training settings.
summary: Age-predicted maximal heart rate formulas, even those tailored to specific populations, have significant variation across age groups, limiting their applicability in exercise training.

content: For most previously sedentary clients with CVD, the threshold intensity for improving cardiorespiratory fitness can occur at as little as 42%–55% V̇ O2R (25), performed for 25–40 minutes, 3 d ∙ wk−1 (~800 kcal ∙ wk−1).
summary: For sedentary clients with CVD, improving cardiorespiratory fitness can begin at 42-55% V̇ O2R, performed for 25-40 minutes, 3 days per week.

content: Increasing the volume of activity (i.e., 3,000 kcal ∙ wk−1), whether by intensity, duration, frequency, or a combination of all three can promote greater improvements in fitness, improve metabolic profile, and weight loss in obese patients (26).
summary: Increasing exercise volume (3,000 kcal/week) through intensity, duration, frequency, or a combination can improve fitness, metabolic profile, and weight loss in obese patients.

content: The overarching goal of the aerobic exercise program for all patients is to have a steady progression of total weekly energy expenditure ≥1,500 kcal ∙ wk−1 (27).
summary: The goal of aerobic exercise programs is to achieve a steady progression of weekly energy expenditure of at least 1,500 kcal.

content: Resistance training in patients with CVD improves muscular strength and endurance, decreases cardiovascular demands of a given task, helps prevent/treat other chronic diseases (e.g., osteoporosis, Type 2 diabetes), increases ability to perform daily activities, and improves self-confidence, among other benefits.
summary: Resistance training for patients with CVD improves muscular strength, endurance, cardiovascular health, and overall well-being.

content: Two primary goals of resistance training for those with CVD are to maintain and improve muscular fitness levels for performing activities of daily living (ADL) and to reduce the cardiovascular demands (e.g., lower heart rate and blood pressure) associated with performing these tasks.
summary: Two main goals of resistance training for CVD patients are maintaining/improving muscular fitness for ADLs and reducing cardiovascular demands during these tasks.

content: Resistance training is widely considered to be safe in patients with well-controlled CVD, provided that proper form through movements are followed while the Valsalva maneuver and tight griping of weight handles/bars are avoided.
summary: Resistance training is generally safe for well-controlled CVD patients, provided proper form is used and Valsalva maneuvers and tight gripping are avoided.

content: Generally, deconditioned patients are recommended to first perform roughly 4 weeks of aerobic training to adjust to and tolerate exercise and then begin incorporating resistance training.
summary: Deconditioned patients should initially perform 4 weeks of aerobic training before starting resistance training.

content: Progression of resistance training should be gradual, but often, the Personal Trainer may need to accept maintenance of strength as a more realistic objective.
summary: Resistance training progression should be gradual, and maintaining strength may be a more realistic goal for some patients.

content: Exercise should be terminated if signs or symptoms such as dizziness, arrhythmias, unusual shortness of breath, or chest discomfort occur.
summary: Exercise should be stopped if dizziness, arrhythmias, shortness of breath, or chest discomfort occur.

content: Dynamic muscle-strengthening exercises include machine and free weights, weight-bearing calisthenics, resistance bands, and similar resistance exercises that use major muscle groups.
summary: Dynamic muscle-strengthening exercises include machines, free weights, calisthenics, and resistance bands.

content: Isometric exercise is not recommended for clients with CVD.
summary: Isometric exercise is not recommended for clients with CVD.

content: Resistance training should be performed on 2–3 nonconsecutive days per week.
summary: Resistance training should be performed 2-3 nonconsecutive days per week.

content: Perform 10–15 repetitions per exercise to “moderate” fatigue, which approximately corresponds to an RPE range of 11–13 (light to somewhat hard) on the Borg 6–20 scale.
summary: Perform 10-15 repetitions per exercise to moderate fatigue, corresponding to an RPE of 11-13.

content: Complete one set of each exercise initially and build up to three sets.
summary: Begin with one set per exercise and progress to three sets.

content: Perform 8–10 different exercises focused on major muscle groups.
summary: Perform 8-10 exercises targeting major muscle groups.

content: Allow adequate rest between exercises to prevent carry over fatigue.
summary: Allow sufficient rest between exercises to prevent carryover fatigue.

content: Increase slowly as patient adapts (~1–5 lb ∙ wk−1 [1–2.3 kg] for upper body and 5–10 lb ∙ wk −1 [2.3–4.5 kg] for lower body as tolerated).
summary: Gradually increase weight as tolerated, with 1-5 lb/week for upper body and 5-10 lb/week for lower body.

content: Roughly 16% of patients undergoing a CVD-related procedure undergo a sternotomy.
summary: Approximately 16% of patients undergoing CVD procedures undergo sternotomy.

content: The Personal Trainer is encouraged to inquire with the medical team or cardiac rehabilitation staff (if the client attended cardiac rehabilitation) about limitations one should be aware of when designing the resistance training program.
summary: Personal trainers should consult with medical teams or cardiac rehabilitation staff regarding limitations for resistance training programs.

content: Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder stemming from abnormal pancreatic insulin production and/or diminished peripheral action of insulin.
summary: Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder characterized by abnormal insulin production and/or action.

content: Diabetes is positively associated with the development of multiple diseases and disorders of the heart, vascular system (e.g., stroke and hypertension), kidneys, eyes, and nervous system.
summary: Diabetes increases the risk of various diseases, including heart disease, stroke, kidney disease, eye problems, and nervous system disorders.

content: Diabetes seriously compromises the heart and vascular system such that it is listed as the seventh leading cause of death in the United States.
summary: Diabetes is a major risk factor for death, ranking as the seventh leading cause of death in the United States.

content: Patients with CVD and Type 2 diabetes have a fourfold higher incidence rate of cardiovascular events compared to patients who only have CVD.
summary: Patients with both CVD and Type 2 diabetes have a significantly higher risk of cardiovascular events compared to those with only CVD.

content: To date, 34.2 million American adults (10.5% of the U.S. adult population) have diabetes mellitus.
summary: Approximately 34.2 million American adults have diabetes mellitus, representing 10.5% of the adult population.

content: Of that total, 7.3 million undiagnosed cases for adults reported in 2018.
summary: In 2018, 7.3 million adults in the U.S. had undiagnosed diabetes.

content: Two categories are used to classify individuals with diabetes: Type 1 and Type 2.
summary: Diabetes is classified into two types: Type 1 and Type 2.

content: Type 1 diabetes typically results from an autoimmune response whereby the body’s own immune system mistakenly destroys the insulin-producing cells in the pancreas.
summary: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune disease where the body attacks insulin-producing cells in the pancreas.

content: Type 1 diabetes comprises approximately 5%–10% of all diagnosed cases of diabetes.
summary: Type 1 diabetes accounts for 5-10% of all diagnosed diabetes cases.

content: To date, 34.2 million American adults (10.5% of the U.S. adult population) have diabetes mellitus. Of that total, 7.3 million undiagnosed cases for adults reported in 2018. Two categories are used to classify individuals with diabetes: Type 1 and Type 2. Type 1 diabetes typically results from an autoimmune response whereby the body’s own immune system mistakenly destroys the insulin-producing cells in the pancreas. Type 1 diabetes comprises approximately 5%–10% of all diagnosed cases of diabetes. This leaves 90%–95% of all diagnosed adults in the category of Type 2 diabetes.
summary: Diabetes prevalence and types: 34.2 million Americans have diabetes, with Type 1 comprising 5-10% and Type 2 the majority.

content: The critical risk factors for Type 2 diabetes are associated with a sedentary lifestyle, whereas the primary risk factors include age, family history, ethnicity, obesity, high alcohol intake, high-fat diet, high blood triglycerides (TGs), high blood pressure, and gestational diabetes.
summary: Risk factors for Type 2 diabetes:  lifestyle, age, genetics, obesity, diet, and other health conditions.

content: It is estimated that approximately 84.1 million adult Americans (33.9% of the U.S. adult population) and nearly half of adults 65 years or older have prediabetes, a condition in which blood glucose values are elevated beyond normal levels (e.g., fasting blood glucose of 100–126 mg ∙ dL−1); these individuals have a markedly increased risk of developing Type 2 diabetes in their lifetime.
summary: Prediabetes prevalence: 84.1 million adults in the U.S. have prediabetes, significantly increasing their risk of developing Type 2 diabetes.

content: Although Type 2 diabetes is more commonly associated with adults, it is now on the rise in children, fueled largely by inactivity and poor diets that lead to obesity.
summary: Rising Type 2 diabetes in children:  Inactivity and poor diets contribute to increasing cases in younger populations.

content: Diabetes prevention is appropriate for all populations not only for the individuals who are obese but also for the older adults and at-risk children.
summary: Diabetes prevention:  Important for all populations, including obese individuals, older adults, and children at risk.

content: Because the growth of diabetes in the U.S. population is not slowing, the demand for competent Personal Trainers to provide appropriate exercise guidance and supervision to individuals with diabetes will continue to increase. Personal Trainers need to prepare to meet this challenge by continuing to enhance their ability to implement diabetes management and prevention programs.
summary: Growing demand for diabetes-specialized trainers:  The increasing prevalence of diabetes necessitates Personal Trainers with specialized knowledge in diabetes management and prevention.

content: Normally, insulin is released in the pancreas in response to a rise in blood glucose following the intake and digestion of food. In Type 1 diabetes, pancreatic β-cells that produce insulin are destroyed by an autoimmune disorder, creating an absolute insulin deficiency (no insulin production) in the body. In Type 2 diabetes, insulin is produced but is ineffective at controlling blood glucose because of insulin resistance in body tissues. The pancreas increases insulin production to overcome this resistance, causing an excess of blood insulin in these individuals.
summary: Insulin function in diabetes: Type 1 involves insulin deficiency due to autoimmune destruction, while Type 2 involves insulin resistance and overproduction.

content: Hyperinsulinemia (elevated blood insulin concentration) over time can contribute to a host of problems such as hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, excessive blood clotting, atherosclerosis, and kidney stones.
summary: Complications of hyperinsulinemia:  Elevated blood insulin can lead to various health issues like high blood pressure, cholesterol, clotting, and kidney problems.

content: The main goal in the management of diabetes is adequately controlling blood glucose levels. Normal resting blood glucose level is less than 100 mg ∙ dL−1. Diabetes is typically diagnosed when fasting blood glucose is 126 mg ∙ dL−1 or greater on two or more occasions or a 2-hour plasma glucose ≥200 mg ∙ dL−1 during an oral glucose tolerance test or glycolated hemoglobin (HbA1C) ≥6.5% or symptoms of hyperglycemia/hyperglycemic crisis and a random plasma glucose ≥200 mg ∙ dL−1.
summary: Diabetes diagnosis and management:  Controlling blood glucose levels is key, with diagnosis based on fasting glucose, oral glucose tolerance test, or HbA1C levels.

content: Although blood glucose numbers describe a single point in time, the HbA1C provides a better measure of glucose control over the last 2–3 months. In people without diabetes, a normal HbA1C value is somewhere between 3.5% and 5.5%. People with diabetes have higher HbA1C values because their bodies have consistently higher levels of blood glucose. A goal level for HbA1C for most people with diabetes is under 7%.
summary: HbA1C as a measure of long-term glucose control:  HbA1C reflects average blood glucose over 2-3 months, with a target below 7% for most diabetics.

content: Exercise, among other treatment strategies, can be used effectively to achieve this goal. Exercise training affects many subclinical health factors associated with diabetes and is critical for diabetes management.
summary: Exercise as a diabetes management tool:  Exercise positively impacts various health factors and is crucial for managing diabetes.

content: The main exercise programming goals for individuals with diabetes are to improve insulin sensitivity and blood glucose control and decrease insulin requirements, improve cardiorespiratory fitness, improve blood lipid profiles, reduce blood pressure, improve muscular strength and endurance through enhancing skeletal muscle mass, improve flexibility and joint range of motion, reduce body weight (particularly reduce intra-abdominal fat), and assist with decreasing the risk of diabetic complications.
summary: Exercise goals for individuals with diabetes:  Improve insulin sensitivity, blood glucose control, cardiorespiratory fitness, lipid profiles, blood pressure, muscular strength, flexibility, body weight, and reduce diabetic complications risk.

content: Consistency in a daily routine is the major pillar in diabetes care. This regularity refers to when meals are eaten; the amount and type of food; when medications are taken; and frequency, intensity, and time (duration and time of day) of physical activity.
summary: Importance of consistency in diabetes care:  Regularity in meals, medications, and exercise is crucial for effective diabetes management.

content: Personal Trainers, when working with clients with diabetes, should maintain regular contact with the client’s physician or other health care provider when designing or making changes to the exercise program. This will enable a more consistent and appropriate treatment plan for the client.
summary: Collaboration with healthcare providers:  Personal Trainers should work closely with clients' doctors to ensure appropriate exercise programs for diabetes management.

content: The majority of research regarding exercise training and diabetes has been done in the area of aerobic exercise. A hallmark training adaptation to be expected from increased levels of aerobic activity is improved cardiorespiratory fitness. Individuals with higher levels of cardiorespiratory fitness are at decreased risk for mortality from CVD compared with their counterparts with lower cardiorespiratory fitness levels regardless of body mass index (BMI) status (normal, overweight, or obese) (34,35).
summary: Aerobic exercise and diabetes:  Research primarily focuses on aerobic exercise, which improves cardiorespiratory fitness and reduces CVD risk.

content: The positive effects of aerobic exercise on glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity in clients with diabetes are known to be ‘subacute’ changes, meaning that they are lost within a few days following the cessation of training (32). For Personal Trainers, this provides support for a consistent, almost daily training regimen for clients with diabetes. If these clients are to achieve the full benefits of aerobic exercise, the program must involve frequent exercise activities with daily adherence.
summary: Benefits of consistent aerobic exercise:  Positive effects on glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity are temporary, necessitating frequent exercise for sustained benefits.

content: Regular exercise in clients with diabetes assists in controlling blood glucose, enhancing insulin sensitivity, decreasing and managing body weight and blood pressure, improving lipid profiles, increasing cardiorespiratory fitness and exercise capacity, and managing some related conditions such as coronary heart disease or peripheral vascular disease (12,32).
summary: Benefits of exercise for diabetes management:  Improved blood glucose, insulin sensitivity, weight, blood pressure, lipid profiles, cardiorespiratory fitness, and management of related conditions.

content: ACSM recommends 3–7 d ∙ wk−1 with no more than 2 consecutive days between sessions of aerobic activity because of the relatively brief exercise-induced improvements in insulin action (32). Greater frequencies of physical activity have been shown to be more effective in improving glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity with minimal exercise-induced complications (36). Personal Trainers should consider progressing clients to 5 d ∙ wk−1, or perhaps daily, with an appropriate mix of intensity and duration.
summary: Exercise frequency recommendations:  ACSM recommends 3-7 days of aerobic activity per week, with greater frequencies being more effective for glucose control and insulin sensitivity.

content: If these clients are to achieve the full benefits of aerobic exercise, the program must involve frequent exercise activities with daily adherence. Regular exercise in clients with diabetes assists in controlling blood glucose, enhancing insulin sensitivity, decreasing and managing body weight and blood pressure, improving lipid profiles, increasing cardiorespiratory fitness and exercise capacity, and managing some related conditions such as coronary heart disease or peripheral vascular disease (12,32). ACSM recommends 3–7 d ∙ wk−1 with no more than 2 consecutive days between sessions of aerobic activity because of the relatively brief exercise-induced improvements in insulin action (32). Greater frequencies of physical activity have been shown to be more effective in improving glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity with minimal exercise-induced complications (36). Personal Trainers should consider progressing clients to 5 d ∙ wk−1, or perhaps daily, with an appropriate mix of intensity and duration. Clients who are obese or are taking insulin may benefit most by a daily schedule because it allows for greater consistency and an opportunity for increasing caloric expenditure for purposes of weight management (32).
summary: Frequent aerobic exercise, ideally daily, is recommended for clients with diabetes to maximize benefits like blood glucose control, insulin sensitivity, and weight management.

content: ACSM recommends a range of 40%–59% of V̇ O2R or HRR for clients with diabetes (12). In the case when exercise testing data is not available, subjective ratings of exertion should be at “moderate” to “very hard.” Individual health status is an important consideration when selecting initial exercise intensity. In individuals who are regular exercisers, better blood glucose control may be achieved at higher exercise intensities (≥60% V̇ O2R), and these clients may be well suited for HIIT (12). For clients who are overweight, sedentary, and/or more deconditioned, an appropriate starting point is 40% of V̇ O2R or HRR or slightly lower depending on the initial fitness level and tolerance to exercise. The decision to progress the client through the intensity range is to be made after taking into consideration the client’s age, his or her ability to tolerate exercise, and his or her individual goals. In general, frequency and duration goals should be realized before implementing a significant progression in intensity.
summary: Exercise intensity for clients with diabetes should be individualized, ranging from 40%-59% of V̇ O2R or HRR, and adjusted based on fitness level, goals, and tolerance.

content: ACSM recommends that both Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes clients accumulate a total of at least 150 minutes of moderate- to vigorous-intensity activity per week (12). However, because many individuals with diabetes are also obese, gradually increasing exercise time to 300 min ∙ wk−1 is recommended to facilitate improvements in glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity and potential changes in body weight (36). This amount may seem high for the client who is overweight, deconditioned, or older; however, exercise may be performed in multiple bouts throughout the day to achieve the overall exercise time goal (12,32). Progressing up to 30–40 minutes of continuous activity will help the client achieve his or her caloric expenditure goals.
summary: Exercise duration recommendations for clients with diabetes are at least 150 minutes per week, but increasing to 300 minutes is beneficial for weight management and glucose control.

content: Guidelines for choosing a mode of exercise are similar to those for an apparently healthy adult. In general, program adherence is improved if the client chooses an exercise modality that he or she enjoys. Walking is the most common form of exercise for clients with diabetes, however, for clients who are obese or experience diabetic complications (peripheral neuropathy is one), Personal Trainers should minimize high-impact, weight-bearing activities or those that require greater balance and coordination (12). Therefore, alternating weight-bearing activities with non–weight-bearing activities, such as cycling, upper body ergometry, and swimming, may enhance the safety and appropriateness of the exercise program.
summary: Exercise modality selection should consider client preferences and any limitations due to diabetes complications, prioritizing enjoyable activities and alternating between weight-bearing and non-weight-bearing options.

content: Increasing cardiorespiratory fitness and maximizing caloric expenditure are of high priority in clients with Type 2 diabetes (5). Thus, the Personal Trainer should progressively increase exercise duration (either continuous or accumulated) and develop a program that promotes beneficial adaptations while combating boredom.
summary: Exercise programs for clients with Type 2 diabetes should prioritize increasing cardiorespiratory fitness and caloric expenditure through progressive duration increases and engaging activities.

content: The ACSM resistance training recommendations for healthy individuals (see Chapter 14) are applicable for people with either prediabetes or Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes, with the understanding that unique contraindications to resistance exercise exist in this population — A resistance training program is essential for clients with diabetes to assist in managing their disease and associated complications as well as maintaining their physiological function through improving strength and endurance. Some data suggests that the increased risk of diabetes with increasing age is partly related to loss of muscle mass. This age-related muscle atrophy negatively impacts the ability to remain recreationally active, perform ADL, and maintain independence.
summary: Resistance training is crucial for clients with diabetes to manage their condition, improve strength and endurance, and combat age-related muscle loss.

content: Resistance training should be encouraged for individuals with diabetes or prediabetes in the absence of contraindications, such as uncontrolled hypertension, severe proliferative retinopathy, and recent treatments using laser surgery. Higher resistance (i.e., heavier weight) may be beneficial for optimization of skeletal muscle strength, insulin action, and blood glucose control, although moderate resistance may be equally effective in previously sedentary individuals.
